<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**Define the Pyroelectric Effect. Name any two materials exhibiting this effect.**

**Model Answer:**
The pyroelectric effect is the phenomenon whereby certain non-centrosymmetric crystals develop a temporary surface electric charge in response to a change in temperature. The temperature variation alters the spontaneous polarization $P_s$ of the crystal, releasing surface charge proportional to $p \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$, where $p$ is the pyroelectric coefficient, $A$ is electrode area, and $\Delta T$ is the temperature change.

Two pyroelectric materials:
1. **Lithium Tantalate ($LiTaO_3$)** — used in commercial PIR motion sensors.
2. **Lead Zirconate Titanate (PZT)** — ceramic with the highest $p$ value.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 each for the two materials with relevance.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]
**What is Curie Temperature? Why is it important in the design of pyroelectric sensors?**

**Model Answer:**
The Curie temperature ($T_c$) is the temperature above which a pyroelectric crystal loses its spontaneous polarization and transitions to a non-polar (paraelectric) phase, becoming unresponsive to thermal stimuli.

**Design Importance:**
- The sensor must be operated well **below $T_c$** to retain its piezoelectric/pyroelectric properties.
- For PZT, $T_c = 230$ °C, so a safety margin (e.g., 80 °C operating ceiling) is built into datasheet ratings.
- Materials with low $T_c$ (e.g., TGS at 49 °C) are unsuitable for high-temperature or self-heating applications.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for design relevance.)*

---

## Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

### Question A — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

#### (a) Explain with a neat diagram the working principle of a pyroelectric sensor. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

A pyroelectric sensor consists of a thin (10–50 μm) wafer of a pyroelectric crystal (commonly $LiTaO_3$) with electrodes deposited on opposite faces. A black absorbing layer (gold black) coats the front face to maximize IR absorption. The element is mounted in a hermetically sealed metal can (TO-5 package) with an IR-transparent window (silicon or germanium) and integrated with a JFET source-follower for impedance buffering.

**Working Principle (5 steps):**

1. **Incident IR Energy:** Chopped or pulsed IR radiation from a human body (~9.4 μm), flame, or laser falls on the absorbing surface.
2. **Temperature Rise:** The absorber converts photons to heat, raising the crystal temperature by a small $\Delta T$.
3. **Polarization Change:** $\Delta T$ reduces the spontaneous polarization $P_s$ of the crystal by an amount $\Delta P_s = p \cdot \Delta T$.
4. **Charge Liberation:** This polarization change releases surface charges $Q = p \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ on the electrodes.
5. **Signal Output:** The charges flow as a current $i_p = p \cdot A \cdot (dT/dt)$ into the JFET, which buffers it to a usable voltage output.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    IR1[IR Radiation]:::s --> ABS1[Black Absorber]
    ABS1 --> CR1[LiTaO3 Crystal Wafer]
    CR1 --> EL1[Top Electrode]
    CR1 --> EL2[Bottom Electrode]
    EL1 --> FT1[FET Buffer]
    EL2 --> FT1
    FT1 --> OT1[Output]

    classDef s fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000
```

**Key Insight:** Since the output is proportional to $dT/dt$, the sensor is **inherently AC-coupled** — it only senses *changing* thermal flux, which is why a mechanical chopper is needed for steady-state IR measurements.

*(7 marks — 3 for diagram, 4 for step-by-step principle.)*

#### (b) Derive the expression for voltage responsivity of a pyroelectric detector. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

Starting from the fundamental charge equation and the equivalent circuit of the sensor, we proceed as derived in Section 3.3 above. The complete derivation yields:

$$R_v(\omega) = \frac{p \cdot A \cdot \omega \cdot R}{\sqrt{1 + \omega^2 \tau_E^2}}$$

where $\tau_E = R \cdot (C_p + C_a)$ is the electrical time constant and $R$ is the parallel combination $R_p \,\vert\vert\, R_a$.

**Frequency-response behavior:**

- **Low-frequency region** ($\omega \ll 1/\tau_E$): $R_v \approx p \cdot A \cdot \omega \cdot R$ — responsivity grows linearly with $\omega$.
- **Roll-off frequency** at $\omega_c = 1/\tau_E$ where the response peaks.
- **High-frequency region** ($\omega \gg 1/\tau_E$): $R_v \approx p \cdot A / (C_p + C_a)$ — a constant, frequency-independent value.

**Design takeaway:** To shift the roll-off to higher frequencies, designers either decrease the load resistance or use a smaller sensor area (lower $C_p$), which is why high-speed laser pulse detectors use sub-millimeter pixels.

*(7 marks — 4 for derivation, 2 for frequency-response analysis, 1 for design takeaway.)*

---

### Question B — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

#### (a) Compare different pyroelectric materials using the concept of figure of merit. Discuss the criteria for material selection. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Figure of Merit (FOM) Concept:**
The FOM eliminates geometric factors (thickness, area) and provides a fair, intrinsic material ranking for either current-mode or voltage-mode operation.

$$F_i = \frac{p}{c \cdot \rho} \qquad\qquad F_v = \frac{p}{c \cdot \rho \cdot \varepsilon_r}$$

**Material Comparison Table:**

| Material | $p$ (C·m⁻²·K⁻¹) | $\varepsilon_r$ | $T_c$ (°C) | FOM (Voltage mode, a.u.) |
|----------|------------------|----------------|------------|--------------------------|
| PZT | $4.0 \times 10^{-4}$ | 1200 | 230 | 0.03 |
| $LiTaO_3$ | $2.3 \times 10^{-4}$ | 47 | 618 | 0.45 |
| TGS | $3.5 \times 10^{-4}$ | 35 | 49 | 0.91 |
| PVDF | $3.0 \times 10^{-5}$ | 12 | 80 | 0.22 |

**Selection Criteria:**

1. **High $p$** — maximizes charge output for a given $\Delta T$.
2. **Low $\varepsilon_r$** — minimizes sensor capacitance, raising $\tau_E$ and voltage responsivity.
3. **High $T_c$** — extends the operating temperature range.
4. **Low dielectric loss** ($\tan \delta$) — reduces Johnson noise, increasing $D^*$.
5. **Mechanical / chemical stability** — long-term drift resistance.
6. **Processability** — single crystals vs. ceramics vs. polymers.

**Conclusion:** $LiTaO_3$ offers the best balance of high $T_c$, moderate $p$, and low $\varepsilon_r$, making it the **industry standard** for commercial PIR sensors.

*(7 marks — 2 for FOM concept, 2 for table, 3 for criteria and conclusion.)*

#### (b) With a block diagram, explain the use of a pyroelectric sensor in a Passive Infrared (PIR) motion detector. List three other applications. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Block Diagram of PIR Motion Detector:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H[Human Body IR ~9.4 um]:::s --> FL[Fresnel Lens Segments]
    FL --> PY[Pyroelectric Element LiTaO3]
    PY --> JF[JFET Source Follower]
    JF --> AM[Two-Stage AC Amplifier]
    AM --> WF[Window Comparator Threshold]
    WF --> RY[Relay or Buzzer Output]

    classDef s fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,color:#000
```

**Operation:**

1. **Optical Collection:** A multi-segment Fresnel lens focuses IR from a moving person onto two sensing zones.
2. **Dual-Element Cancellation:** The detector uses two elements wired in series-opposition. Ambient temperature changes affect both equally and cancel; a moving body crosses zones sequentially, producing a net differential signal.
3. **Signal Conditioning:** The JFET buffers the weak charge pulses; the AC amplifier (gain ~70 dB) boosts them.
4. **Comparator:** A window comparator triggers only when the signal amplitude exceeds a preset threshold within the 0.1–10 Hz band — the typical range of human movement.
5. **Output:** A relay or buzzer is activated to indicate motion.

**Other Applications:**

1. **Flame Detection in Industrial Burners** — detects the 4.3 μm CO₂ emission band of hydrocarbon flames.
2. **FTIR Spectrometers** — high-$D^*$ pyroelectric detectors record the IR interferogram with a few nW of input power.
3. **Non-Contact Infrared Thermometers (Ear/Forehead)** — measures tympanic membrane or forehead thermal emission when a probe-button is pressed (provides the thermal step).

*(7 marks — 3 for block diagram, 2 for operation logic, 2 for three applications.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the time derivative:** Many students write the charge equation $Q = p \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ but fail to state the crucial follow-up that the *current* depends on $dT/dt$ — a steady heat source gives **zero** output. (Lose 2 marks)
> 2. **Mixing up figure-of-merit formulas:** Confusing $F_i$ and $F_v$ definitions. Remember — if the application is voltage-output, $F_v$ is the correct one.
> 3. **Ignoring the role of the chopper:** For continuous IR detection (not pulsed), a mechanical/optical chopper is **mandatory**. Missing this loses 1 mark on application questions.
> 4. **Wrong units in the formula sheet:** $p$ is **not** in C/K — it is **C · m⁻² · K⁻¹**. The area $A$ converts it to total charge. Easy to miswrite under exam pressure.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ Pyroelectric sensors **only respond to changing temperature** ($dT/dt \neq 0$) — a steady heat source produces **zero output**.
- ✅ The fundamental output is **charge**: $Q = p \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$, and the corresponding current is $i_p = p \cdot A \cdot (dT/dt)$.
- ✅ **Curie temperature** is the upper operating limit — operation above $T_c$ permanently destroys polarization.
- ✅ **Figure of merit** — $F_i = p/(c\rho)$ for current mode, $F_v = p/(c\rho\varepsilon)$ for voltage mode — guides material selection.
- ✅ **Voltage responsivity** rolls off above $\omega_c = 1/\tau_E$ where $\tau_E = R(C_p + C_a)$ — smaller area = higher bandwidth.
- ✅ **$LiTaO_3$** is the **industry standard** for PIR motion sensors (best balance of $p$, $T_c$, and $\varepsilon_r$).
- ✅ A **mechanical chopper** is required for measurement of continuous-wave IR radiation.
- ✅ **Dual-element differential configuration** is used in PIR detectors to cancel common-mode ambient temperature drift.
- ✅ Applications to remember: **PIR motion sensors, flame detectors, FTIR spectrometers, laser-pulse profilers, ear thermometers, gas analyzers**.
- ✅ Units to memorize cold: $p$ → **C · m⁻² · K⁻¹**, $D^*$ → **cm · Hz¹ᐟ² · W⁻¹** (Jones).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Photovoltaic Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT721 - SENSORS AND INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Photovoltaic Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Photovoltaic (PV) Sensor** is a solid-state optoelectronic transducer that directly converts incident optical (light) energy into electrical energy through the **photovoltaic effect** — the generation of a voltage across a *p-n junction* upon absorption of photons whose energy is greater than or equal to the band-gap energy of the semiconductor material. The device is operated in the **photovoltaic mode** (zero external bias), where the photo-generated electromotive force (EMF) drives current through an external load without any external power supply.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Photovoltaic sensors belong to the family of *active* (self-generating) sensors. Unlike photoconductive or photodiode sensors, a PV sensor does **not** require an external bias voltage — the *open-circuit voltage* $V_{oc}$ itself is the measurand-related output.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **water reservoir split by a thin wall** with a small gate at the bottom:

- **Photon (rain droplet)** falls on the p-n junction (the thin wall).
- The photon has enough energy to "knock" an electron (water molecule) across the wall, separating charges.
- The **n-side** collects electrons (negative charge), and the **p-side** is left with holes (positive charge).
- The separated charges create a **voltage difference** across the junction — just like water levels rising on one side of the wall.
- Connect a wire (load) across the two sides, and **current flows** — you have a working solar-powered device!

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Summary:** A photovoltaic sensor is a tiny, silent "light-powered battery" — give it photons, and it gives you electricity. The brighter the light, the more current it produces.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Elementary charge:** $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$
- **Boltzmann constant:** $k = 1.381 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J/K}$
- **Thermal voltage at 300 K:** $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q} \approx 25.85 \text{ mV}$
- **Solar spectral peak:** $\lambda \approx 0.55 \;\mu\text{m}$ (visible green)
- **Standard Test Conditions (STC):** Irradiance $G = 1000 \text{ W/m}^2$, AM1.5G spectrum, Cell temp $T = 25^\circ\text{C}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — The Photovoltaic Effect

The phenomenon unfolds in three logical stages:

1. **Photon Absorption:** When a photon of energy $E_{ph} = h\nu$ strikes the semiconductor, and $E_{ph} \geq E_g$ (band-gap energy), it excites an electron from the valence band to the conduction band, creating an **electron-hole pair (EHP)**.
2. **Charge Separation:** The built-in potential barrier of the depletion region at the p-n junction sweeps the electron toward the n-side and the hole toward the p-side, establishing a photovoltage.
3. **Current Generation:** When a load $R_L$ is connected, the photo-generated voltage drives a current $I$ through the circuit, doing useful work.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Why:** Photons below the band-gap energy ($h\nu < E_g$) simply pass through or generate heat — they **cannot** produce an EHP. This is why *material selection* is critical.

### 2.2 Equivalent Circuit Model of a PV Cell

An ideal PV cell is modelled as a **current source** (light-generated current $I_L$) in **parallel** with a **p-n junction diode**, with the entire combination supplying a load $R_L$. Real cells add parasitic resistances: **series resistance $R_s$** (contact/bulk ohmic losses) and **shunt resistance $R_{sh}$** (leakage across the junction).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Quantity / Parameter** | **Equation** | **Units** | **Remarks** |
|:-----:|:-------------------------|:-------------|:---------:|:------------|
| 1 | Photon energy | $E_{ph} = h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | J (or eV) | $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s |
| 2 | Cut-off wavelength | $\lambda_c = \dfrac{hc}{E_g} = \dfrac{1.24}{E_g(\text{eV})}$ | $\mu$m | Wavelength above which no EHP is generated |
| 3 | Shockley PV equation (ideal) | $I = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV}{kT}\right) - 1 \right]$ | A | Fundamental IV relation |
| 4 | Thermal voltage | $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q}$ | V | $\approx 25.85$ mV at 300 K |
| 5 | Short-circuit current | $I_{sc} = I_L \quad (\text{when } V=0)$ | A | Linear with irradiance $G$ |
| 6 | Open-circuit voltage | $V_{oc} = \dfrac{kT}{q} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0} + 1\right)$ | V | Logarithmic with $G$ |
| 7 | Maximum power | $P_m = V_m \cdot I_m$ | W | Product at MPP |
| 8 | Fill Factor | $FF = \dfrac{V_m \cdot I_m}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}$ | dimensionless | Typically 0.6 – 0.85 |
| 9 | Conversion efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_m}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{FF \cdot V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}{G \cdot A}$ | dimensionless | $A$ = cell area |
| 10 | Ideal efficiency limit (SQ) | $\eta_{SQ} \approx 33.7\%$ | — | Shockley–Queisser limit for single junction |
| 11 | Series resistance effect | $\Delta V_{oc} \approx I \cdot R_s$ | V | Reduces fill factor |
| 12 | Shunt resistance effect | $\Delta I \approx \dfrac{V}{R_{sh}}$ | A | Reduces $I_{sc}$ and slope near $V_{oc}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** $I_{sc}$ is a **linear** function of irradiance $G$ (double the light, double the current), but $V_{oc}$ is a **logarithmic** function of $G$ (dim light still produces a usable voltage). This is why PV cells are excellent for low-light operation.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Renewable energy generation:** Solar farms, rooftop PV modules.
- **Precision light measurement:** Photometers, lux meters, exposure meters in photography.
- **Position/alignment sensors:** Solar trackers in heliostats and satellites.
- **Isolated signal sensing:** Optocouplers, optical encoders, and line-follower robots.
- **Space applications:** Satellite power systems (Gallium Arsenide PV cells dominate due to high $\eta$ and radiation tolerance).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Open-Circuit Voltage $V_{oc}$

**Starting Point:** The general Shockley diode equation for a PV cell:

$$I = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV}{kT}\right) - 1 \right]$$

**Step 1 — Apply the Open-Circuit Condition:**
At open circuit, the load terminals are disconnected, so the **net external current $I = 0$**, while the terminal voltage equals the open-circuit voltage $V_{oc}$.

$$0 = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV_{oc}}{kT}\right) - 1 \right]$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to Isolate the Exponential Term:**

$$I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV_{oc}}{kT}\right) - 1 \right] = I_L$$

**Step 3 — Divide Both Sides by $I_0$:**

$$\exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV_{oc}}{kT}\right) - 1 = \dfrac{I_L}{I_0}$$

**Step 4 — Add 1 to Both Sides:**

$$\exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV_{oc}}{kT}\right) = \dfrac{I_L}{I_0} + 1$$

**Step 5 — Take the Natural Logarithm of Both Sides:**

$$\dfrac{qV_{oc}}{kT} = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0} + 1\right)$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $V_{oc}$:**

$$V_{oc} = \dfrac{kT}{q} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0} + 1\right) = V_T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0} + 1\right)$$

> This is the **fundamental $V_{oc}$ equation** the KTU examiner expects. Note that since $I_L \gg I_0$ under typical illumination, the "+1" inside the logarithm is often omitted for approximation, giving $V_{oc} \approx V_T \ln(I_L / I_0)$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Photocurrent $I_L$ in Terms of Irradiance

**Step 1 — Photon flux incident on the cell of area $A$:**

$$\Phi = \dfrac{G \cdot A}{E_{ph}} = \dfrac{G \cdot A \cdot \lambda}{hc}$$

**Step 2 — Number of EHPs generated per second (assuming quantum efficiency $\eta_q$):**

$$N_{EHP} = \eta_q \cdot \Phi = \eta_q \cdot \dfrac{G \cdot A \cdot \lambda}{hc}$$

**Step 3 — Photo-generated current is charge per unit time:**

$$I_L = q \cdot N_{EHP} = \dfrac{q \cdot \eta_q \cdot G \cdot A \cdot \lambda}{hc}$$

This shows that $I_L \propto G$ — a **strictly linear** relationship, which is essential for using PV cells as calibrated light-intensity sensors.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Parameter Sensitivity Analysis

| **Parameter Changed** | **Effect on $I_{sc}$** | **Effect on $V_{oc}$** | **Effect on $FF$** | **Effect on $\eta$** |
|:----------------------|:----------------------:|:---------------------:|:-----------------:|:-------------------:|
| ↑ Irradiance $G$ | ↑↑ Linear | ↑ Logarithmic | ≈ Slight ↑ | ↑↑ Strongly |
| ↑ Temperature $T$ | ↑ Slight (bandgap shrinks) | ↓↓ Linear ($V_{oc} \approx -2 \text{ mV/}^\circ\text{C}$ per cell) | ↓ | ↓↓ |
| ↑ $R_s$ (series R) | ≈ No change | ↓ | ↓↓ Steeply | ↓↓ |
| ↓ $R_{sh}$ (shunt R) | ↓ | ≈ No change | ↓ | ↓ |
| ↑ Band-gap $E_g$ | ↓ (fewer absorbed photons) | ↑ (higher barrier) | varies | Trade-off peak |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** The temperature coefficient of $V_{oc}$ is **negative** — silicon PV cells lose roughly **0.4–0.5 % efficiency per °C** rise above 25 °C. This is a guaranteed exam favourite.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Physical Layer Diagram — A Photovoltaic Cell (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    LightIn["Incident Sunlight Photon Flux"]:::input
    AntiRefl["Anti-Reflection Coating Si3N4 Layer"]:::layer
    TopGrid["Top Metallic Contact Grid Fingers"]:::layer
    NLayer["n-Type Emitter Layer Heavily Doped N+"]:::layer
    Depletion["P-N Junction Depletion Region Built-in Electric Field"]:::core
    PLayer["p-Type Base Layer Lightly Doped P"]:::layer
    BackCont["Back Metallic Contact Aluminum"]:::layer
    Load["External Load Resistor R_L"]:::output
    VoltageOut["Output Terminal Voltage Voc across load"]:::output

    LightIn --> AntiRefl
    AntiRefl --> TopGrid
    TopGrid --> NLayer
    NLayer --> Depletion
    Depletion --> PLayer
    PLayer --> BackCont
    BackCont --> Load
    Load --> VoltageOut
    NLayer -. carries electrons .-> VoltageOut
    PLayer -. carries holes .-> VoltageOut

    classDef input fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef layer fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#1E3A5F,color:#000
    classDef core fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFF
    classDef output fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Equivalent Circuit View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LightG["Light Source Irradiance G"]:::src
    PhotocurrentSrc["Photo-Generated Current Source I_L"]:::main
    DiodeD["Ideal P-N Junction Diode D with Saturation Current I_0"]:::main
    Rsh["Shunt Resistance R_sh Parallel Leakage Path"]:::parasitic
    Rs["Series Resistance R_s Contact and Bulk Ohmic Losses"]:::parasitic
    LoadRL["Load Resistor R_L External Circuit"]:::load
    Vout["Terminal Voltage V across Load"]:::meas

    LightG --> PhotocurrentSrc
    PhotocurrentSrc --- NodeA["Node A"]
    DiodeD --- NodeA
    Rsh --- NodeA
    NodeA --> Rs --> Vout
    Vout --> LoadRL
    LoadRL --> NodeB["Ground Reference Node B"]
    NodeB --- PhotocurrentSrc

    classDef src fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000
    classDef main fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    classDef parasitic fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#555,color:#000
    classDef load fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef meas fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Equivalent Circuit:**
> - The **current source $I_L$** is controlled by the light level.
> - The **diode D** represents the inherent p-n junction of the cell.
> - **$R_{sh}$** is the unintended leakage path (ideally $\infty$).
> - **$R_s$** is the unintended series ohmic loss (ideally 0).
> - **$R_L$** is the *useful* external load where output voltage $V$ appears.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] Define the photovoltaic effect. How does it differ from the photoconductive effect?**

**Model Answer:**

The **photovoltaic effect** is the phenomenon of generating a voltage across a *p-n junction* upon absorption of incident photons, leading to a separation of photo-generated electron-hole pairs by the junction's built-in electric field. The device acts as a self-generating source and operates without external bias.

| **Feature** | **Photovoltaic Effect** | **Photoconductive Effect** |
|:------------|:-----------------------:|:--------------------------:|
| Bias required | No (zero bias) | Yes (external bias) |
| Output | Voltage / Current | Change in conductance |
| Active/Passive | Active sensor | Passive sensor |
| Linearity | $I_{sc}$ linear with $G$ | Resistance nonlinear with $G$ |

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for difference table, 1 for active/passive classification)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] What is the fill factor of a photovoltaic cell? Why is it always less than unity?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Fill Factor (FF)** is the ratio of the *actual maximum power* delivered by the cell to the product of its *open-circuit voltage* and *short-circuit current*:

$$FF = \dfrac{V_m \cdot I_m}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}$$

It is **always less than unity** because:
1. The IV curve is not rectangular — the rounded shape between $I_{sc}$ and $V_{oc}$ means the maximum power rectangle is smaller than the bounding box.
2. Internal **series resistance $R_s$** causes a voltage drop, while **shunt resistance $R_{sh}$** causes current leakage, both shrinking the usable area.
3. The diode saturation current $I_0$ creates a "knee" near $V_{oc}$ that bends the curve.

Typical FF for silicon cells = **0.7 to 0.85**.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for causes)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the construction and working principle of a photovoltaic (solar) cell. Discuss the role of the depletion region in charge separation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the open-circuit voltage $V_{oc}$ of a PV cell from the basic Shockley equation. A silicon PV cell has a photo-generated current $I_L = 2.5 \text{ A}$ and reverse saturation current $I_0 = 10^{-10} \text{ A}$ at 300 K. Calculate $V_{oc}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Construction & Working (7 marks):**

A PV cell is fabricated as a single-crystal silicon wafer with a shallow **n+ emitter** diffused on a **p-type base**. The top surface has an **anti-reflection coating (ARC)** of Si₃N₄ or TiO₂ to minimize reflection losses, and a **metallic grid** (typically silver) for current collection. The back has a continuous **aluminum contact**.

**Working principle — step by step:**

1. **Photon absorption:** Photons with $h\nu \geq E_g$ (1.12 eV for Si) penetrate the ARC and enter the depletion region.
2. **EHP generation:** Each absorbed photon creates one electron-hole pair.
3. **Charge separation:** The **built-in electric field** of the depletion region (≈ 10⁵ V/cm) sweeps electrons to the n-side and holes to the p-side, preventing recombination.
4. **Voltage build-up:** Accumulated charges produce a forward-biasing potential that opposes the built-in field, establishing equilibrium.
5. **Current extraction:** When a load is connected, the photo-generated EMF drives current through it. The terminal voltage is the open-circuit voltage $V_{oc}$ when no current flows.

**Role of depletion region:** Acts as the *engine* of the PV cell — without it, photo-generated EHPs would simply recombine and produce no useful output.

*(Marks split: 2 diagram description + 3 step-by-step working + 2 role of depletion region)*

---

**(b) Derivation + Numerical (7 marks):**

**Derivation (already done in Section 3.1) — final result:**

$$V_{oc} = \dfrac{kT}{q} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0} + 1\right)$$

**Numerical Computation:**

Given: $I_L = 2.5 \text{ A}$, $I_0 = 10^{-10} \text{ A}$, $T = 300 \text{ K}$.

$$V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q} = \dfrac{1.381 \times 10^{-23} \times 300}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}}$$

$$V_T = \dfrac{4.143 \times 10^{-21}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = 0.02586 \text{ V} \approx 25.86 \text{ mV}$$

Now compute the logarithm:

$$\dfrac{I_L}{I_0} = \dfrac{2.5}{10^{-10}} = 2.5 \times 10^{10}$$

Since $I_L / I_0 \gg 1$, the "+1" inside the ln is negligible:

$$V_{oc} \approx V_T \ln\!\left(2.5 \times 10^{10}\right)$$

$$\ln\!\left(2.5 \times 10^{10}\right) = \ln(2.5) + 10 \ln(10) = 0.9163 + 23.0259 = 23.9422$$

Therefore:

$$V_{oc} = 0.02586 \times 23.9422 = 0.6192 \text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{V_{oc} \approx 0.619 \text{ V}}$$

*(Marks split: 3 derivation + 1 thermal voltage calculation + 2 numerical substitution + 1 final answer with units)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Alternative Set]**

**(a)** Draw the equivalent circuit of a real photovoltaic cell and explain the significance of series resistance $R_s$ and shunt resistance $R_{sh}$. How do they affect the fill factor? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define *conversion efficiency* of a PV cell. A PV module has $V_{oc} = 21 \text{ V}$, $I_{sc} = 3.5 \text{ A}$, and fill factor $FF = 0.75$. The module area is $0.5 \text{ m}^2$ and it receives $800 \text{ W/m}^2$ irradiance. Calculate the conversion efficiency. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Equivalent Circuit (7 marks):**

The real PV cell equivalent circuit (as shown in Section 4.2) consists of:
- A current source $I_L$ (photo-generated)
- A diode $D$ (p-n junction)
- Shunt resistance $R_{sh}$ in parallel
- Series resistance $R_s$ in series
- Connected to a load $R_L$

**Significance of $R_s$:** Represents the *ohmic resistance* of the semiconductor bulk, the metallic contacts, and the inter-cell connections. Ideally, $R_s = 0$.

**Significance of $R_{sh}$:** Represents the *leakage current path* across the junction due to manufacturing defects, impurities at the edges, and surface recombination. Ideally, $R_{sh} = \infty$.

**Effect on Fill Factor:**
- ↑ $R_s$ → voltage drop across the series path → $V_m$ falls → **FF decreases sharply**.
- ↓ $R_{sh}$ → current leaks around the diode → $I_m$ falls → **FF decreases**.

In a high-quality cell, $R_s < 1 \text{ }\Omega$ and $R_{sh} > 1000 \text{ }\Omega$.

*(Marks split: 2 circuit drawing + 2 Rs significance + 2 Rsh significance + 1 FF effect)*

---

**(b) Efficiency Numerical (7 marks):**

**Definition:** Conversion efficiency $\eta$ is the ratio of electrical power output at the maximum power point to the incident optical power on the cell area.

$$\eta = \dfrac{P_m}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{FF \cdot V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}{G \cdot A}$$

**Given:**
- $V_{oc} = 21 \text{ V}$
- $I_{sc} = 3.5 \text{ A}$
- $FF = 0.75$
- $A = 0.5 \text{ m}^2$
- $G = 800 \text{ W/m}^2$

**Step 1 — Maximum Power:**

$$P_m = FF \cdot V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} = 0.75 \times 21 \times 3.5$$

$$P_m = 0.75 \times 73.5 = 55.125 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2 — Incident Power:**

$$P_{in} = G \cdot A = 800 \times 0.5 = 400 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3 — Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \dfrac{55.125}{400} = 0.1378$$

$$\boxed{\eta \approx 13.78\%}$$

*(Marks split: 1 definition + 1 formula + 1 step 1 + 1 step 2 + 2 step 3 + 1 final answer with units and percentage conversion)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in PV Sensor Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing modes:** Students often state that a PV cell is "always reverse-biased" — it is **zero-biased (photovoltaic mode)**. Reverse-biasing makes it a *photodiode*, not a PV sensor.
> 2. **Wrong sign of temperature coefficient:** $V_{oc}$ **decreases** with temperature (≈ −2 mV/°C per cell). Writing "increases" is a guaranteed 1-mark cut.
> 3. **Forgetting "+1" in ln:** Strictly, the formula is $\ln(I_L/I_0 + 1)$, not $\ln(I_L/I_0)$. Examiners often award 0.5 marks for missing the "+1" if $I_L/I_0$ is huge, but you may lose the full mark in rigorous problems.
> 4. **Confusing $I_{sc}$ with $I_L$:** $I_{sc} = I_L$ only in the *ideal* case. With shunt leakage, $I_{sc} < I_L$.
> 5. **Forgetting units in numerical answers:** Always state V, A, W, or % explicitly.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ PV sensors are **active, self-generating** transducers operating in **zero-bias** mode.
- ✅ The **photovoltaic effect** is the voltage generated across a p-n junction under illumination.
- ✅ **$I_{sc} \propto G$** (linear) — makes PV cells excellent linear light-intensity sensors.
- ✅ **$V_{oc} = V_T \ln(I_L/I_0 + 1)$** — logarithmic dependence on light and exponential on temperature.
- ✅ **Fill Factor $FF = (V_m I_m) / (V_{oc} I_{sc})$** — typically 0.7–0.85; always < 1.
- ✅ **Efficiency $\eta = (FF \cdot V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}) / (G \cdot A)$** — silicon cells reach ~20–26%, GaAs up to ~30% (multijunction even higher).
- ✅ **Series resistance $R_s$** hurts FF; **shunt resistance $R_{sh}$** hurts $I_{sc}$.
- ✅ **Temperature effect:** $V_{oc}$ decreases ≈ 2 mV/°C; $I_{sc}$ increases slightly.
- ✅ **Cut-off wavelength:** $\lambda_c = 1.24 / E_g(\text{eV}) \;\mu\text{m}$ — for Si, $\lambda_c \approx 1.11 \;\mu\text{m}$.
- ✅ **Shockley–Queisser limit:** Maximum theoretical efficiency for a single-junction cell ≈ **33.7%**.
- ✅ **Common materials:** Si (1.12 eV), GaAs (1.42 eV), CdTe (1.45 eV), CIGS (1.1 eV), perovskites (tunable 1.2–2.3 eV).
- ✅ **Applications:** Solar power, light meters, optical encoders, optocouplers, satellite power, line-follower robots, sun-trackers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrochemical Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT721 - SENSORS AND INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Electrochemical Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

An **Electrochemical Sensor** is a class of chemical sensor that transduces the chemical information (concentration of an analyte, partial pressure, or activity of a specific ion) into an analytically useful electrical signal, by exploiting the electrochemical reaction occurring at the interface between an electrode and an electrolyte.

According to the **IUPAC (International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry)** definition, an electrochemical sensor is a device that provides real-time, quantitative, and selective analytical information using a recognition element (chemical or biological) integrated with an electrochemical transducer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Focus:** Students must master four classical electrochemical sensor families — **Potentiometric**, **Amperometric**, **Conductometric (Impedimetric)**, and **Voltammetric** — along with the underlying electrochemistry (Nernst Equation, Faraday's Laws).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Salted Battery" Intuition

Imagine a lemon battery you might have built in school. You insert two different metal strips (zinc and copper) into a lemon, and a voltage appears between them. Now imagine the *strength* of that voltage depends not on the lemon, but on how much **salt (NaCl)** you dissolved in the water around the metals.

- The **lemon juice** (electrolyte) carries charge.
- The **metal strips** (electrodes) sense what's happening.
- The **voltage** that appears is the **electrical signal**.

An electrochemical sensor works on the *same exact principle*, but engineered to be exquisitely selective to one specific chemical species (e.g., glucose, pH, oxygen, CO₂). The "salt" in the analogy is replaced by the **analyte** (the substance being measured).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** In an electrochemical sensor, the **analyte participates in a redox (oxidation–reduction) reaction** at an electrode surface, and this reaction is converted into a measurable voltage, current, or impedance change.

### 1.3 Fundamental Physical Constants

| Constant | Symbol | Standard Value | Importance |
|----------|--------|----------------|------------|
| Faraday Constant | $F$ | **96,485 C mol⁻¹** | Links charge to moles of electrons |
| Universal Gas Constant | $R$ | **8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹** | Nernst equation base |
| Standard Temperature | $T$ | **298.15 K (25 °C)** | Standard state reference |
| Thermal Voltage Factor | $RT/F$ | **0.025693 V** | At 25 °C |

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners frequently test the numerical value of $F$ and $RT/F$ — memorize **$F \approx 96,485$ C mol⁻¹** and **$RT/F \approx 0.0257$ V** at STP.

### 1.4 Classification Overview

Electrochemical sensors are broadly classified by the **electrical parameter** they measure:

1. **Potentiometric** — measures *potential (voltage)* at zero current.
2. **Amperometric** — measures *current* at a fixed applied potential.
3. **Conductometric / Impedimetric** — measures *conductance* or *impedance*.
4. **Voltammetric** — measures *current* as a function of *swept potential*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

Every electrochemical sensor operates on a unified three-stage process:

- **Stage 1 — Recognition:** The target analyte selectively interacts with a recognition layer (ion-selective membrane, enzyme, gas-permeable membrane, etc.) at the electrode–electrolyte interface.
- **Stage 2 — Transduction:** A redox or charge-transfer event occurs, producing an electrical perturbation (potential, current, or impedance).
- **Stage 3 — Signal Output:** The electrical signal is conditioned, amplified, and displayed, providing a quantitative measure of analyte concentration.

### 2.2 Type 1 — Potentiometric Sensors

- **Mechanism:** Measures the open-circuit potential (zero current flow) developed across an ion-selective membrane.
- **Reference:** A stable reference electrode (e.g., SCE — Saturated Calomel Electrode, or SHE — Standard Hydrogen Electrode) is used.
- **Working:** Indicator electrode develops a potential proportional to the logarithm of ion activity.
- **Why it works:** The Nernst equation governs the equilibrium potential.

**KTU High-Yield Formula:**

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$

At $T = 298.15$ K, in base-10 log form:

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{0.05916}{n} \log_{10} \frac{a_{\text{Red}}}{a_{\text{Ox}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The famous **glass pH electrode** is a potentiometric sensor where $E = E^{\circ} - 0.05916 \cdot \text{pH}$ at 25 °C.

### 2.3 Type 2 — Amperometric Sensors

- **Mechanism:** A fixed potential is applied between working and reference electrodes, and the resulting steady-state current is measured.
- **Why fixed potential?** It drives a specific electrochemical reaction at a mass-transport-limited rate.
- **Cottrell Equation (governs transient current):**

$$i(t) = \frac{n F A D^{1/2} C_b}{\pi^{1/2} t^{1/2}}$$

- **Real-world utility:** Glucose biosensors, dissolved oxygen sensors (Clark electrode), and toxic gas detectors (e.g., CO, H₂S) all use amperometric transduction.

### 2.4 Type 3 — Conductometric / Impedimetric Sensors

- **Mechanism:** Measures the change in solution conductivity or interfacial impedance.
- **Why it works:** Ionic concentration directly affects bulk resistance.
- **Conductance formula:**

$$G = \frac{1}{R} = \frac{\kappa \cdot A}{l}$$

where $\kappa$ is the specific conductance (S m⁻¹), $A$ is the cross-sectional area, and $l$ is the electrode spacing.

### 2.5 Type 4 — Voltammetric Sensors

- **Mechanism:** Applies a linearly varying potential and records the resulting current.
- **Why sweep the potential?** It identifies analytes by their characteristic peak potential (like a chemical "fingerprint").
- **Randles–Sevcik Equation (peak current for reversible systems):**

$$i_p = (2.69 \times 10^{5})\, n^{3/2} A D^{1/2} C \, v^{1/2}$$

where $v$ is the scan rate (V s⁻¹).

### 2.6 The Three-Electrode Cell (Universal Architecture)

> [!NOTE]
> Every modern electrochemical sensor employs a **three-electrode electrochemical cell**:
> - **Working Electrode (WE):** Site of the redox reaction (Au, Pt, Glassy Carbon, Hg).
> - **Reference Electrode (RE):** Maintains a stable, known potential (Ag/AgCl, SCE, SHE).
> - **Counter (Auxiliary) Electrode (CE):** Completes the circuit and carries the current.

### 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Name | Used In | Key Variable |
|---|---------|------|---------|--------------|
| 1 | $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$ | **Nernst Equation** | Potentiometric | $E$ = cell potential |
| 2 | $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{0.05916}{n} \log_{10} Q$ | Nernst (25 °C) | Potentiometric | Base-10 form |
| 3 | $m = \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$ | **Faraday's 1st Law** | Amperometric | $m$ = mass deposited |
| 4 | $i(t) = \dfrac{n F A D^{1/2} C_b}{\pi^{1/2} t^{1/2}}$ | **Cottrell Equation** | Amperometric | Transient diffusion current |
| 5 | $i_p = (2.69 \times 10^{5}) n^{3/2} A D^{1/2} C v^{1/2}$ | **Randles–Sevcik** | Voltammetric | Peak current |
| 6 | $G = \kappa A / l$ | Conductance | Conductometric | Cell constant = $l/A$ |
| 7 | $\eta = E - E_{eq}$ | Overpotential | All types | Polarization driving force |
| 8 | $I = n F A k C$ | Kinetic current | Amperometric | $k$ = rate constant |

> [!WARNING]
> **Unit trap in KTU exams:** When using Nernst, ensure $Q$ is **dimensionless** (use activities, not concentrations) and $n$ is the **number of electrons transferred per mole** of reaction.

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Biomedical:** Blood glucose meters (amperometric), arterial blood gas analyzers (potentiometric), pulse oximeters (optical cousin).
- **Environmental:** pH meters for water quality, dissolved oxygen probes in lakes, heavy-metal ion detectors.
- **Industrial:** Process control in chemical plants, corrosion monitoring via linear polarization resistance.
- **Automotive:** Lambda ($\lambda$) oxygen sensors in catalytic converters (potentiometric zirconia-based).
- **Defense/Safety:** Electrochemical toxic gas detectors (HCN, Cl₂, NH₃).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Nernst Equation (from Thermodynamic Foundation)

The Nernst equation is the cornerstone of every potentiometric sensor. We will derive it rigorously from the **Gibbs free energy**.

**Step 1 — Start with the general redox half-reaction:**

$$\text{Ox} + n e^{-} \rightleftharpoons \text{Red}$$

**Step 2 — Write the change in Gibbs free energy for the electrochemical process:**

The electrical work done moving $n$ moles of electrons through potential $E$ is $W = -nFE$.

By the first and second laws of thermodynamics for an electrochemical cell at constant $T$ and $P$:

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$

But also, the maximum non-expansion work is:

$$\Delta G = -n F E \quad \text{and} \quad \Delta G^{\circ} = -n F E^{\circ}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and equate the two expressions for $\Delta G$:**

$$-nFE = -nFE^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$

**Step 4 — Divide both sides by $-nF$ (the negative sign flips the log term):**

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$

**Step 5 — Convert natural log to base-10 log using $\ln x = 2.303 \log_{10} x$:**

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{2.303 \, RT}{nF} \log_{10} Q$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the constant at $T = 298.15$ K:**

$$\frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298.15}{96485} = 0.05916 \text{ V}$$

**Final Nernst Equation (at 25 °C):**

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{0.05916}{n} \log_{10} \frac{a_{\text{Red}}}{a_{\text{Ox}}}$$

This is the **generalized Nernst equation** that every KTU potentiometric question is based on.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — The pH Electrode Response Equation

The glass pH electrode is the most commercially successful potentiometric sensor in human history. Let us derive its specific response law.

**Step 1 — Apply Nernst to the half-reaction occurring at the glass membrane:**

For the H⁺ ion reaction: $2\text{H}^{+} + 2e^{-} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}_2$, $n = 2$:

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{0.05916}{2} \log_{10} \frac{a_{\text{H}_2}}{a_{\text{H}^{+}}^{2}}$$

**Step 2 — For a glass electrode, the partial pressure of H₂ is held constant (atmospheric), so the only variable is $a_{\text{H}^{+}}$:**

$$E = \text{constant} + \frac{0.05916}{1} \log_{10} a_{\text{H}^{+}}$$

**Step 3 — Use the definition of pH: $\text{pH} = -\log_{10} a_{\text{H}^{+}}$:**

$$E = \text{constant} - 0.05916 \cdot \text{pH}$$

**Final pH Electrode Equation:**

$$E_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ\prime} - 0.05916 \cdot \text{pH}$$

This is a **linear** relationship — a 1-unit pH change produces a **59.16 mV** change in cell potential at 25 °C.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** **"59 mV per pH unit per electron"** — this number appears in almost every KTU paper on potentiometric sensors.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis

**Step 1 — Total charge passed through the cell:**

$$Q = I \cdot t$$

**Step 2 — Number of moles of electrons transferred:**

$$n_{e^-} = \frac{Q}{F} = \frac{I \cdot t}{F}$$

**Step 3 — By stoichiometry, moles of substance reacted (with $n$ electrons per molecule):**

$$n_{\text{substance}} = \frac{n_{e^-}}{n} = \frac{I \cdot t}{n F}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to mass using molar mass $M$:**

$$m = M \cdot n_{\text{substance}} = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$$

**Final Faraday's First Law:**

$$m = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$$

This is the foundation of all **amperometric** and **coulometric** sensors, including the Clark oxygen sensor.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Cottrell Equation (Diffusion-Limited Current)

**Step 1 — Fick's second law for semi-infinite linear diffusion:**

$$\frac{\partial C(x,t)}{\partial t} = D \frac{\partial^2 C(x,t)}{\partial x^2}$$

with initial condition $C(x,0) = C_b$ and boundary conditions $C(0,t) = 0$ (instant consumption at electrode) and $C(\infty,t) = C_b$.

**Step 2 — The solution (error function) is:**

$$C(x,t) = C_b \cdot \text{erf}\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Fick's first law gives the flux at the electrode surface ($x = 0$):**

$$J(0,t) = -D \left.\frac{\partial C}{\partial x}\right|_{x=0} = \frac{D C_b}{\sqrt{\pi D t}} = C_b \sqrt{\frac{D}{\pi t}}$$

**Step 4 — Current density is $j = nFJ$, and total current $i = j \cdot A$:**

$$i(t) = n F A C_b \sqrt{\frac{D}{\pi t}}$$

**Final Cottrell Equation:**

$$i(t) = \frac{n F A D^{1/2} C_b}{\pi^{1/2} t^{1/2}}$$

This shows that **current decays as $1/\sqrt{t}$** under pure diffusion control — a key signature tested in KTU amperometric problems.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (for exam quick revision)

| Property | Potentiometric | Amperometric | Conductometric | Voltammetric |
|----------|----------------|--------------|----------------|--------------|
| Signal measured | Voltage (V) | Current (A) | Conductance (S) | Current vs. V |
| Applied potential | None (zero current) | Fixed | AC excitation | Linear sweep |
| Governing equation | Nernst | Faraday / Cottrell | $G = \kappa A / l$ | Randles–Sevcik |
| Selectivity | High (membrane-based) | Moderate | Poor (total ions) | High (peak position) |
| Typical example | pH glass electrode | Clark O₂ sensor | Salinity probe | Heavy-metal analysis |
| Sensitivity | ~59 mV/decade | Linear with conc. | Linear with $\kappa$ | $i_p \propto C$ |
| KTU mark weight | **Highest** | **High** | Medium | Medium |

> [!TIP]
> In KTU 2024 pattern questions, the term "Nernst" almost always means **potentiometric**, and "Cottrell" almost always means **amperometric**. This saves time in part-A 3-markers.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture — Electrochemical Sensor System

The following Mermaid block-diagram maps the complete functional flow of a generic electrochemical sensor from analyte contact to digital readout. It is engineered to comply with the strict alphanumeric-node and clean-label rules of the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Target Analyte in Sample] --> A2[Diffusion to Sensor Surface]
    A2 --> A3[Selective Recognition Layer]
    A3 --> A4[Working Electrode Interface]
    A4 --> A5[Redox Reaction or Charge Transfer]
    A5 --> A6[Electrical Signal Generation]
    A6 --> A7{Signal Type Selector}
    A7 -->|Voltage| A8[Potentiometric Channel]
    A7 -->|Current| A9[Amperometric Channel]
    A7 -->|Conductance| A10[Conductometric Channel]
    A8 --> A11[Signal Conditioning Amplifier]
    A9 --> A11
    A10 --> A11
    A11 --> A12[Analog to Digital Converter]
    A12 --> A13[Microcontroller Processing]
    A13 --> A14[Display and Data Output]
    A5 -.reference path.-> A15[Reference Electrode SHE or AgAgCl]
    A5 -.current return.-> A16[Counter Electrode Pt]
    A15 --> A7
    A16 --> A7
```

### 4.2 Three-Electrode Cell Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Working Electrode WE] --> B2[Potentiostat Circuit]
    B3[Reference Electrode RE] --> B4[High Impedance Voltmeter]
    B5[Counter Electrode CE] --> B6[Ammeter Current Path]
    B2 --> B7[Electrolyte Solution]
    B4 --> B7
    B6 --> B7
    B7 --> B1
    B7 --> B3
    B7 --> B5
    B2 --> A11
    B4 --> A11
    B6 --> A11
```

### 4.3 Classification Decision Tree (for exam quick classification)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[Electrochemical Sensor] --> C2{What is measured?}
    C2 -->|Voltage at zero current| C3[Potentiometric]
    C2 -->|Current at fixed V| C4[Amperometric]
    C2 -->|Impedance or Conductance| C5[Conductometric]
    C2 -->|Current vs swept V| C6[Voltammetric]
    C3 --> C3a[pH electrode]
    C3 --> C3b[Ion Selective Electrode]
    C3 --> C3c[Lambda Oxygen Sensor]
    C4 --> C4a[Clark Oxygen Sensor]
    C4 --> C4b[Glucose Biosensor]
    C4 --> C4c[Toxic Gas Detector]
    C5 --> C5a[Salinity Probe]
    C5 --> C5b[Total Dissolved Solids]
    C6 --> C6a[Cyclic Voltammetry]
    C6 --> C6b[Differential Pulse Voltammetry]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality Check:** In real KTU lab kits, the **three-electrode setup with a Potentiostat** (e.g., CHI 600E, Gamry, or Autolab) is universal. Students should be familiar with the role of the **Potentiostat** as the "brain" that controls WE-vs-RE potential and measures current at CE.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define an electrochemical sensor. With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a potentiometric sensor.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An electrochemical sensor is a device that converts the concentration of a chemical species into a measurable electrical signal (voltage, current, or impedance) via a redox reaction at an electrode–electrolyte interface.

A potentiometric sensor measures the **open-circuit potential** developed at an indicator electrode relative to a **reference electrode**, with **zero current** flowing. The relationship is governed by the **Nernst equation**:

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{0.05916}{n} \log_{10} \frac{a_{\text{Red}}}{a_{\text{Ox}}}$$

The classic example is the **glass pH electrode**, where $E = E^{\circ\prime} - 0.05916 \cdot \text{pH}$ at 25 °C, giving 59.16 mV per pH unit.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often write "current is zero" without mentioning **open-circuit** conditions. KTU examiners deduct ½ mark for this missing term.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*State Faraday's laws of electrolysis. How are they relevant to amperometric sensors?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Faraday's First Law:** The mass of a substance liberated at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of charge passed:

$$m = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$$

**Faraday's Second Law:** The mass liberated is proportional to the equivalent weight of the substance.

**Relevance to Amperometric Sensors:** In amperometric sensors (e.g., Clark O₂ sensor), a fixed potential drives a redox reaction. The resulting **current is directly proportional to the concentration** of the analyte. By measuring the current and integrating over time, the **total charge** passed can be used to determine the **moles** of analyte via Faraday's law, and hence its concentration:

$$Q = n F N \quad \Rightarrow \quad C = \frac{Q}{n F V}$$

where $V$ is the sample volume.

---

### 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU End Semester Examinations (ESE) in the 2024 scheme mandate an **internal choice** between two 14-mark questions from each module. Both are presented below.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Potentiometric Focus**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**
**(a)** Derive the Nernst equation from thermodynamic principles. Show that at 25 °C, $E = E^{\circ} - (0.05916/n) \log_{10} Q$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A glass electrode is used to measure the pH of an unknown solution. The cell EMF measured is **0.412 V**, and the standard cell potential $E^{\circ\prime} = 0.546$ V. Calculate the pH of the solution. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to (a) — Full Step-by-Step (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the relationship between Gibbs free energy and cell potential.

$$\Delta G = -nFE \quad \text{and} \quad \Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Write the general isotherm for the redox reaction $\text{Ox} + ne^{-} \rightleftharpoons \text{Red}$:

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Substitute the electrical work expressions:

$$-nFE = -nFE^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Divide by $-nF$:

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Convert natural log to base-10:

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{2.303 \, RT}{nF} \log_{10} Q$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Substitute standard values $R = 8.314$ J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹, $T = 298.15$ K, $F = 96485$ C mol⁻¹:

$$\frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298.15}{96485} = 0.05916 \text{ V}$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Final equation:

$$\boxed{E = E^{\circ} - \frac{0.05916}{n} \log_{10} \frac{a_{\text{Red}}}{a_{\text{Ox}}}}$$

---

**Solution to (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Recall the pH electrode equation:

$$E_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ\prime} - 0.05916 \cdot \text{pH}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Rearrange for pH:

$$\text{pH} = \frac{E^{\circ\prime} - E_{\text{cell}}}{0.05916}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Substitute given values $E^{\circ\prime} = 0.546$ V, $E_{\text{cell}} = 0.412$ V:

$$\text{pH} = \frac{0.546 - 0.412}{0.05916}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Compute the numerator:

$$0.546 - 0.412 = 0.134 \text{ V}$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Divide and compute:

$$\text{pH} = \frac{0.134}{0.05916} = 2.265$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** State the final answer with units:

$$\boxed{\text{pH} \approx 2.27}$$

The solution is **acidic** (pH < 7), as expected.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Amperometric Focus**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]**
**(a)** Explain the construction and working of a **Clark Oxygen Sensor** with a neat diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In an amperometric experiment, a constant potential of $-0.7$ V vs. Ag/AgCl is applied to a working electrode of area $A = 0.05$ cm². The diffusion coefficient is $D = 7.0 \times 10^{-6}$ cm² s⁻¹, and the bulk concentration of the electroactive species is $C_b = 1.2$ mM. Assuming a 2-electron reduction ($n = 2$), compute the **Cottrell current** at $t = 5$ s after the potential step. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to (a) — Full Descriptive (7 marks):**

**Construction (3 marks):**

The Clark oxygen sensor consists of:
- A **cathode** (working electrode) — typically Pt or Au, area ~ 0.05 cm².
- An **anode** (counter + reference) — Ag/AgCl.
- A **KCl electrolyte** solution trapped between the electrodes.
- A **gas-permeable membrane** (e.g., PTFE/Teflon or polypropylene) of thickness ~ 10–25 μm that separates the electrolyte from the sample.
- A potentiostat circuit applies a polarizing voltage of **$-0.7$ V** vs. Ag/AgCl.

**Working (4 marks):**

1. Dissolved O₂ from the sample diffuses through the **gas-permeable membrane** into the thin electrolyte film.
2. At the cathode, O₂ is **reduced** in an acid electrolyte:

$$\text{O}_2 + 4\text{H}^{+} + 4e^{-} \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

3. At the Ag/AgCl anode, oxidation occurs:

$$\text{Ag} + \text{Cl}^{-} \rightarrow \text{AgCl} + e^{-}$$

4. The **reduction current is proportional to the dissolved O₂ concentration**, following the Cottrell/Faraday relationships.
5. The current is diffusion-limited through the membrane, so the sensor response is **linear with $p_{O_2}$**.

---

**Solution to (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the Cottrell equation:

$$i(t) = \frac{n F A D^{1/2} C_b}{\pi^{1/2} t^{1/2}}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Convert all units to CGS (cm-s):
- $n = 2$
- $F = 96{,}485$ C mol⁻¹ $= 96{,}485$ A·s mol⁻¹
- $A = 0.05$ cm²
- $D = 7.0 \times 10^{-6}$ cm² s⁻¹
- $C_b = 1.2$ mM $= 1.2 \times 10^{-6}$ mol cm⁻³
- $t = 5$ s

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Compute $D^{1/2}$:

$$D^{1/2} = \sqrt{7.0 \times 10^{-6}} = 2.646 \times 10^{-3} \text{ cm s}^{-1/2}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Compute $t^{1/2}$:

$$t^{1/2} = \sqrt{5} = 2.236 \text{ s}^{1/2}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Compute the denominator $\pi^{1/2} t^{1/2}$:

$$\pi^{1/2} \cdot t^{1/2} = 1.7725 \times 2.236 = 3.963$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Substitute all values into the numerator:

$$n F A D^{1/2} C_b = 2 \times 96{,}485 \times 0.05 \times 2.646 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.2 \times 10^{-6}$$

Compute step-by-step:

$$2 \times 96{,}485 = 192{,}970$$

$$192{,}970 \times 0.05 = 9{,}648.5$$

$$9{,}648.5 \times 2.646 \times 10^{-3} = 25.532$$

$$25.532 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-6} = 3.064 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Final division and answer:

$$i(5) = \frac{3.064 \times 10^{-5}}{3.963} = 7.73 \times 10^{-6} \text{ A}$$

$$\boxed{i(5) \approx 7.73 \, \mu\text{A}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit conversion trap:** If $C_b$ is left in mM without converting to mol cm⁻³, the answer will be off by a factor of **$10^3$** or **$10^6$**. Always convert.
> 2. **Forgetting $n$:** Many students use $n = 1$ even when the problem says "2-electron reduction." This leads to a 50% error.
> 3. **Confusing $i$ vs $j$:** KTU papers sometimes ask for **current density** $j = i/A$. Read carefully.
> 4. **Sign convention:** Reduction currents at the cathode are conventionally **negative**, but KTU accepts magnitude. Write "$\vert i \vert = 7.73 \, \mu\text{A}$" to be safe.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Block — Read this the night before the KTU exam!**

- [x] **Definition:** Electrochemical sensor = chemical → electrical transducer via redox reaction.
- [x] **Four Types:** Potentiometric (V), Amperometric (I), Conductometric (G), Voltammetric (I vs. V).
- [x] **Nernst Equation:** $E = E^{\circ} - (RT/nF) \ln Q = E^{\circ} - (0.05916/n) \log_{10} Q$ at 25 °C.
- [x] **pH Electrode:** $E = E^{\circ\prime} - 0.05916 \cdot \text{pH}$ → **59 mV per pH unit**.
- [x] **Faraday's 1st Law:** $m = (M \cdot I \cdot t)/(n \cdot F)$, with **$F = 96{,}485$ C mol⁻¹**.
- [x] **Cottrell Equation:** $i(t) = n F A D^{1/2} C_b / (\pi^{1/2} t^{1/2})$ — current $\propto 1/\sqrt{t}$.
- [x] **Randles–Sevcik:** $i_p = (2.69 \times 10^{5}) n^{3/2} A D^{1/2} C v^{1/2}$ for reversible voltammetry.
- [x] **Three-electrode cell:** WE (Pt/Au/GC), RE (Ag/AgCl or SCE), CE (Pt wire).
- [x] **Clark Sensor:** Membrane-covered amperometric O₂ sensor, polarizing voltage **$-0.7$ V**.
- [x] **Glucose Sensor:** GOx enzyme + amperometric H₂O₂ detection at **+0.6 V** vs. Ag/AgCl.
- [x] **Lambda Sensor:** Potentiometric ZrO₂ oxygen sensor in automotive catalytic converters.
- [x] **Key Constants:** $F = 96{,}485$ C mol⁻¹, $R = 8.314$ J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹, $RT/F = 0.0257$ V at 25 °C.
- [x] **KTU Mnemonic for Sensor Types — "PVC Volcano":** **P**otentiometric, **V**oltammetric, **C**onductometric / **A**mperometric. (P, V, C, A — order of decreasing selectivity).
- [x] **Selectivity Order:** Voltammetric ≈ Potentiometric > Amperometric > Conductometric.
- [x] **Always convert** mM to mol cm⁻³ (= mol L⁻¹) when using Cottrell in CGS units.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Static Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT721 - SENSORS AND INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Static Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Static Characteristics** of a sensor or measurement instrument are the set of performance attributes that describe the behaviour of the device when the **input quantity is either constant (steady-state) or varying very slowly with time**, so that the inertial, dynamic, and transient effects become negligible.

In simpler examiner-language: when the measurand is "frozen in time" and you read the corresponding output, the relationship between that input and the resulting output is governed entirely by the **static characteristics** of the instrument. These are obtained from the **static calibration curve** — a plot of output versus input under equilibrium conditions.

The principal static characteristics prescribed by the KTU OEERT721 syllabus are:

1. **Accuracy**
2. **Precision**
3. **Sensitivity (Static Sensitivity)**
4. **Linearity (and Non-linearity)**
5. **Hysteresis**
6. **Repeatability**
7. **Reproducibility**
8. **Resolution**
9. **Threshold**
10. **Dead Zone / Dead Band**
11. **Drift (Zero Drift, Span Drift, Zener Drift)**
12. **Span** and **Range**
13. **Input Impedance** and **Loading Effect**

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Bathroom Weighing Scale 🧍‍♂️⚖️**
> 
> Imagine you step on a digital weighing scale and the display shows **68.4 kg**. Now think about *everything that single number is telling you*:
> 
> - **Accuracy** — How close is 68.4 kg to your *true* weight? If you actually weigh 69.0 kg on a doctor's beam balance, the bathroom scale has a 0.6 kg error.
> - **Precision** — If you step on and off the scale 10 times, how tightly clustered are the readings? Five readings of 68.4, 68.5, 68.4, 68.3, 68.4 = high precision.
> - **Sensitivity** — For every extra 1 kg you put on, by how much does the displayed number change? (e.g., 1.0 count/kg).
> - **Hysteresis** — Step on the scale gently vs. step on it heavily from a jump. Do you get the same number for the same 68 kg load?
> - **Resolution** — The display jumps in steps of 0.1 kg. That minimum step is the resolution.
> - **Threshold** — Place a 10 g paperweight on it. Does the display even budge from zero? If not, 10 g is below threshold.
> - **Drift** — Leave the scale "empty" for an hour. Does it still read **0.0 kg**, or has it crept to **0.1 kg**? That creep is zero drift.

Every one of these descriptors — accuracy, precision, sensitivity, hysteresis, resolution, threshold, drift — is a **static characteristic** because we are observing the scale under a *stationary* load.

---

## 1.3 Static vs Dynamic — The Critical Distinction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Favourite Distinction:**
> 
> - **Static characteristics** ↔ input is **time-invariant** (DC behaviour) → described by algebraic equations.
> - **Dynamic characteristics** ↔ input is **time-varying** (AC behaviour) → described by differential equations (first-order, second-order, time-constant, damping ratio, natural frequency, etc.).
> 
> The *same* sensor has **two personalities**: a static personality (governed by gain, offset, linearity) and a dynamic personality (governed by inertia, damping, bandwidth).

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Static Calibration Curve — Foundation of Everything

When a known input $x_i$ is applied to an instrument and held constant, the steady output $y_o$ is recorded. Plotting $y_o$ versus $x_i$ for the full range of the instrument gives the **static calibration curve** (or *input–output characteristic curve*). The **ideal** instrument has a **straight line** passing through the origin; the **real** instrument deviates from it.

> All static characteristics are mathematical descriptors of *how*, *where*, and *by how much* the actual calibration curve deviates from the ideal straight line.

---

## 2.2 Detailed Theoretical Breakdown

### (A) Accuracy 🎯
- **Definition:** The closeness of the *measured* value to the *true* value of the measurand.
- **Mathematical form:**
$$A = \left(1 - \frac{\vert e \vert}{x_{true}}\right) \times 100\,\%$$
where $e = x_{measured} - x_{true}$ is the absolute error.
- **Why it matters:** The single most quoted specification in any datasheet. KTU problems often express accuracy as a **% of Full Scale (FS)** or **% of Reading (% RDG)**.

### (B) Precision 🔬
- **Definition:** The degree of *reproducibility* of measurements — how tightly the readings cluster when the same input is applied repeatedly under identical conditions.
- **High precision ≠ High accuracy.** A cluster of readings *far away* from the true value is precise but not accurate.
- Quantified via **standard deviation** $\sigma$ or **variance** $\sigma^2$.

### (C) Sensitivity (Static Sensitivity) 📈
- **Definition:** The ratio of the change in output to the corresponding change in input that caused it.
- **Mathematical form:**
$$S = \frac{\Delta y_o}{\Delta x_i} = \frac{dy_o}{dx_i}$$
- For a linear instrument: $S$ is constant (slope of the calibration line). For a non-linear instrument, sensitivity is the **local slope** at the operating point.
- **Unit:** $\dfrac{\text{unit of output}}{\text{unit of input}}$ (e.g., mV/°C for a thermocouple).

### (D) Linearity 📏
- **Definition:** The constancy of the sensitivity (slope) over the full range of the instrument. A perfectly linear instrument has constant sensitivity everywhere.
- **Independent linearity (%):**
$$\%\,NL = \frac{\vert y_{actual} - y_{ideal\,line} \vert_{max}}{y_{FS}} \times 100\,\%$$
- The **best-fit straight line** (obtained via least-squares regression) is used in KTU problems unless otherwise specified.
- **Types of linearity in the syllabus:** (i) Theoretical/Slope linearity, (ii) Independent linearity, (iii) Zero-based linearity, (iv) Terminal-based linearity.

### (E) Hysteresis 🔄
- **Definition:** The maximum difference in output for the *same* input, depending on whether the input is approached from the **increasing** side (ascending) or the **decreasing** side (descending).
- **Mathematical form:**
$$\%\,H = \frac{\Delta H_{max}}{y_{FS}} \times 100\,\%$$
where $\Delta H_{max}$ is the maximum output difference at any input value between the up-scale and down-scale traverses.
- **Physical causes:** Friction, mechanical backlash, magnetic hysteresis in ferrous cores, elasticity lag in elastic members.

### (F) Repeatability 🔁
- **Definition:** The ability of the instrument to give the *same* output for the *same* input, applied repeatedly under **the same conditions, the same method, the same observer, the same instrument, the same place, and over a short period of time**.
- Expressed as **% of Full Scale**:
$$\%\,R = \frac{\pm\,\Delta R_{max}}{y_{FS}} \times 100\,\%$$
- Repeatability **does NOT include hysteresis** because it is measured on a *single* traverse direction.

### (G) Reproducibility 🌍
- **Definition:** Repeatability measured when the **conditions are intentionally changed** (different observer, different lab, different ambient temperature, different instrument of the same type, long time gap).
- Always **worse** than repeatability because it bundles in additional environmental and human variability.

### (H) Resolution 🔍
- **Definition:** The **smallest measurable change in input** that produces a *detectable* change in output.
- **Mathematical form:**
$$\text{Resolution} = \frac{y_{FS}}{2^n - 1}$$
for an $n$-bit digital instrument. For analog instruments, it is the smallest graduation on the scale.

### (I) Threshold ⚡
- **Definition:** The **smallest input magnitude** that produces a measurable output at all.
- Distinguishing from resolution: below threshold the output is **zero**; above resolution the output **changes by the smallest step**.

### (J) Dead Zone / Dead Band 🚫
- **Definition:** The total range of input values over which the output **does not change** at all.
- Caused by backlash in gears, friction in linkages, and contact potentials.
- Often confused with threshold; in KTU answer-sheets, **dead band ≥ threshold**.

### (K) Drift 🌊
- **Definition:** The undesired change in output over time, *with the input held constant*.
- **Zero drift:** Output shifts at zero input (e.g., the bathroom scale creeping from 0.0 → 0.2 kg overnight).
- **Span (sensitivity) drift:** The *gain* of the instrument changes, so the slope of the calibration curve changes.
- **Zener drift (combined):** Both zero and span shift simultaneously — the whole curve tilts and translates.
- **Thermal drift:** Drift specifically caused by ambient temperature variation; quantified by **temperature coefficient** in ppm/°C.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Table)

| # | Static Characteristic | Mathematical Definition | Standard Unit / % Form | Typical KTU Value Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Sensitivity** $S$ | $S = \dfrac{\Delta y_o}{\Delta x_i}$ | Output unit / Input unit | 1 mV/°C to 10 mV/°C (thermocouple) |
| 2 | **Static Error** $e_s$ | $e_s = y_{actual} - y_{ideal}$ | Same as output | — |
| 3 | **% Non-linearity** | $\%NL = \dfrac{\max \vert y_{act} - y_{ideal line} \vert}{y_{FS}} \times 100$ | Percent of FS | 0.1% – 1% (industrial) |
| 4 | **% Hysteresis** | $\%H = \dfrac{\Delta H_{max}}{y_{FS}} \times 100$ | Percent of FS | 0.5% – 2% |
| 5 | **% Repeatability** | $\%R = \dfrac{\pm \Delta R_{max}}{y_{FS}} \times 100$ | Percent of FS | 0.1% – 0.5% |
| 6 | **% Accuracy (FS)** | $\%A_{FS} = \dfrac{\vert e \vert}{y_{FS}} \times 100$ | Percent of FS | ±0.1% FS (precision), ±2% FS (industrial) |
| 7 | **% Accuracy (RDG)** | $\%A_{RDG} = \dfrac{\vert e \vert}{y_{actual}} \times 100$ | Percent of reading | ±0.5% RDG |
| 8 | **Resolution (digital)** | $R = \dfrac{y_{FS}}{2^n - 1}$ | Output units / LSB | 16-bit → R = 0.0015% FS |
| 9 | **Span** | $\text{Span} = x_{i,max} - x_{i,min}$ | Same as input | 0–200 °C |
| 10 | **Range** | Operating input extent | Same as input | –50 °C to +200 °C |
| 11 | **Drift rate** | $\dot{d} = \dfrac{\Delta y_o}{\Delta t}\bigg\vert_{x_i = const}$ | Output / time | 0.1 °C / month |
| 12 | **Temperature coefficient** | $TC = \dfrac{\Delta y_o / y_o}{\Delta T}$ | ppm/°C | 10 – 100 ppm/°C |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU OEERT721 Module-2 question paper most frequently tests **(i) the definitions of the above characteristics**, **(ii) numerical problems on sensitivity / non-linearity / hysteresis from a given calibration data table**, and **(iii) the distinction between accuracy vs precision, repeatability vs reproducibility, and threshold vs resolution.**

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial process control (pressure transmitters, flow meters):** Accuracy, repeatability, and drift dominate — a 0.1% drift per month on a refinery flow meter translates to crores of rupees in lost product.
- **Medical instrumentation (ECG, blood-pressure cuffs, infusion pumps):** Accuracy and resolution are non-negotiable; lives depend on them.
- **Aerospace (altimeters, pitot tubes, accelerometers):** Hysteresis and temperature drift dominate; aerospace-grade specs push non-linearity below 0.05% FS.
- **Consumer electronics (smartphone IMUs, fitness bands):** Cost-driven; specifications relax to a few % FS but with heavy on-chip digital compensation.
- **Calibration laboratories (national standards, NABL-accredited labs):** Reproducibility is the key metric, as different labs must agree within stipulated uncertainty.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The derivations below show **every algebraic transition** and the **engineering logic** behind each step. KTU problems on Module 2 routinely hand you a calibration data table and ask for sensitivity, non-linearity, hysteresis, or accuracy. The worked example at the end is the *exact* format expected in the answer script.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Best-Fit Straight Line via Least Squares (Background for Linearity Problems)

When the actual calibration curve is non-linear, KTU expects you to draw the **best-fit straight line** through the $(x_i, y_o)$ data points and report non-linearity relative to it.

Let there be $N$ calibration points $(x_1, y_1), (x_2, y_2), \ldots, (x_N, y_N)$. Assume the best-fit line is:

$$y_{fit} = m\,x + c$$

The **sum of squared residuals** to be minimised is:

$$E(m, c) = \sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - (m x_i + c))^2$$

Setting $\dfrac{\partial E}{\partial m} = 0$ and $\dfrac{\partial E}{\partial c} = 0$:

$$\dfrac{\partial E}{\partial m} = -2\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i (y_i - m x_i - c) = 0$$

$$\dfrac{\partial E}{\partial c} = -2\sum_{i=1}^{N} (y_i - m x_i - c) = 0$$

Expanding these two equations:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i y_i = m \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i^2 + c \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i$$

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} y_i = m \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i + N\,c$$

Solving the linear system for $m$ and $c$:

$$m = \frac{N\sum x_i y_i - \left(\sum x_i\right)\left(\sum y_i\right)}{N\sum x_i^2 - \left(\sum x_i\right)^2}$$

$$c = \frac{\sum y_i - m \sum x_i}{N}$$

This slope $m$ is the **average static sensitivity** of the instrument, and the deviation $\vert y_{actual} - y_{fit}\vert$ at every point is used to compute **% non-linearity**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Non-Linearity Index from a Calibration Table

The non-linearity (in % FS) of an instrument whose calibration curve passes through $N$ measured points is:

$$\%\,NL = \frac{\max_{i=1..N}\vert\,y_{i,actual} - y_{i,ideal\,line}\,\vert}{y_{FS}} \times 100\,\%$$

The **ideal line** used in KTU problems is most often the **best-fit straight line** (least-squares), unless the question explicitly says "terminal-based" (line joining the first and last calibration points) or "zero-based" (line from origin through the last point).

**Step-by-step recipe (this is what the examiner awards 7 marks for):**

1. Tabulate the input $x_i$ and observed output $y_i$.
2. Compute the best-fit slope $m$ and intercept $c$ from the least-squares formulas.
3. Compute $y_{fit,i} = m x_i + c$ for every $i$.
4. Compute deviation $D_i = y_i - y_{fit,i}$ at every $i$.
5. Find $D_{max} = \max \vert D_i \vert$.
6. Compute $y_{FS} = y_{max,actual} - y_{min,actual}$.
7. Plug into the formula above to get % NL.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Sensitivity, Hysteresis and Accuracy in One Combined Problem

### Problem (Worked Example)
> A temperature sensor is calibrated against a standard platinum resistance thermometer. The table below gives the output voltage (mV) when the input temperature is increased from 0 °C to 100 °C (Up-scale traverse), and then decreased back from 100 °C to 0 °C (Down-scale traverse). Determine (a) the sensitivity, (b) the % hysteresis, and (c) the % non-linearity (with respect to the best-fit straight line).

| $T$ (°C) | $V_{up}$ (mV) | $V_{down}$ (mV) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.10 | 0.30 |
| 20 | 1.95 | 2.05 |
| 40 | 3.85 | 3.95 |
| 60 | 5.80 | 5.88 |
| 80 | 7.72 | 7.78 |
| 100 | 9.60 | 9.60 |

### Solution

**Part (a) — Sensitivity**

For a *linear* instrument, sensitivity is constant and is taken as the slope between the endpoints of the best-fit line. Using the up-scale data:

$$S = \frac{\Delta V}{\Delta T} = \frac{9.60 - 0.10}{100 - 0} = \frac{9.50}{100}$$

$$S = 0.095 \;\text{mV/°C}$$

So the **static sensitivity** of this sensor is **0.095 mV/°C** (≈ 95 µV/°C — typical of a Type-K thermocouple grade device).

**Part (b) — % Hysteresis**

Compute the up-down difference at every input point:

$$\Delta V_i = V_{up,i} - V_{down,i}$$

| $T$ (°C) | $V_{up}$ | $V_{down}$ | $\Delta V$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.10 | 0.30 | –0.20 |
| 20 | 1.95 | 2.05 | –0.10 |
| 40 | 3.85 | 3.95 | –0.10 |
| 60 | 5.80 | 5.88 | –0.08 |
| 80 | 7.72 | 7.78 | –0.06 |
| 100 | 9.60 | 9.60 | 0.00 |

The maximum absolute difference is $\vert\Delta V_{max}\vert = 0.20$ mV (at $T = 0$ °C). The full-scale output is:

$$V_{FS} = 9.60 - 0.10 = 9.50 \;\text{mV}$$

Therefore the **percentage hysteresis** is:

$$\%H = \frac{0.20}{9.50} \times 100 = 2.105\,\%$$

**Part (c) — % Non-linearity (best-fit line)**

First, apply the least-squares formulas to the **up-scale** data:

$$\begin{aligned}
N &= 6 \\
\sum x_i &= 0+20+40+60+80+100 = 300 \\
\sum y_i &= 0.10+1.95+3.85+5.80+7.72+9.60 = 29.02 \\
\sum x_i^2 &= 0+400+1600+3600+6400+10000 = 22000 \\
\sum x_i y_i &= 0+39+154+348+617.6+960 = 2118.6
\end{aligned}$$

Now compute the slope:

$$\begin{aligned}
m &= \frac{N\sum x_i y_i - (\sum x_i)(\sum y_i)}{N\sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2} \\
&= \frac{6(2118.6) - (300)(29.02)}{6(22000) - (300)^2} \\
&= \frac{12711.6 - 8706.0}{132000 - 90000} \\
&= \frac{4005.6}{42000} \\
&= 0.09537 \;\text{mV/°C}
\end{aligned}$$

And the intercept:

$$\begin{aligned}
c &= \frac{\sum y_i - m \sum x_i}{N} \\
&= \frac{29.02 - (0.09537)(300)}{6} \\
&= \frac{29.02 - 28.611}{6} \\
&= \frac{0.409}{6} \\
&= 0.0682 \;\text{mV}
\end{aligned}$$

So the best-fit line is:

$$V_{fit} = 0.09537\,T + 0.0682$$

Compute the deviation $D_i = V_{up,i} - V_{fit,i}$ at every point:

| $T$ | $V_{up}$ | $V_{fit}$ | $D = V_{up} - V_{fit}$ | $\vert D \vert$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.10 | 0.0682 | +0.0318 | 0.0318 |
| 20 | 1.95 | 1.9756 | –0.0256 | 0.0256 |
| 40 | 3.85 | 3.8830 | –0.0330 | 0.0330 |
| 60 | 5.80 | 5.7904 | +0.0096 | 0.0096 |
| 80 | 7.72 | 7.6978 | +0.0222 | 0.0222 |
| 100 | 9.60 | 9.6052 | –0.0052 | 0.0052 |

The maximum deviation is $D_{max} = 0.0330$ mV (at $T = 40$ °C). Therefore:

$$\%NL = \frac{0.0330}{9.50} \times 100 = 0.347\,\%$$

**Final Answer Summary:**
- Sensitivity $S = 0.095$ mV/°C
- Hysteresis $\%H = 2.11\,\%$ FS
- Non-linearity $\%NL = 0.347\,\%$ FS

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Resolution of an n-bit Digital Instrument

A digital instrument with $n$-bit resolution quantises the full-scale output $y_{FS}$ into $2^n$ discrete levels. The smallest distinguishable step is therefore:

$$\Delta y_{min} = \frac{y_{FS}}{2^n - 1}$$

(Subtracting 1 because the count ranges from $0$ to $2^n - 1$, which gives $2^n - 1$ intervals.) The corresponding **resolution in input units** is:

$$\Delta x_{min} = \frac{\Delta y_{min}}{S} = \frac{y_{FS}}{S(2^n - 1)}$$

**Quick example:** A 12-bit pressure transducer with a full-scale output of 10 V and a sensitivity of 0.05 V/kPa has:

$$\Delta y_{min} = \frac{10}{2^{12}-1} = \frac{10}{4095} = 2.442 \;\text{mV}$$

$$\Delta x_{min} = \frac{2.442\,\text{mV}}{0.05\,\text{V/kPa}} = 0.04884 \;\text{kPa} \approx 48.84 \;\text{Pa}$$

So the **resolution is about 49 Pa**.

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Accuracy and Precision. Why is it possible for an instrument to be precise but not accurate?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Accuracy | Precision |
|---|---|---|
| Meaning | Closeness of the measured value to the **true** value | Closeness of repeated measurements **to each other** |
| Affected by | Systematic errors (calibration error, zero error) | Random errors (noise, friction, observer variability) |
| Quantified by | Absolute or % error relative to true value | Standard deviation / variance of repeated readings |
| Improvement | **Recalibration** against a standard | Better instrumentation, **averaging**, shielding from noise |

An instrument can be **precise but not accurate** if it has a **systematic error** (e.g., a zero offset of +2 °C) that is constant across all readings. The repeated readings cluster tightly (high precision) but the entire cluster is shifted away from the true value (low accuracy). Example: A miscalibrated digital thermometer always reading 2 °C high — every measurement is consistent (precise) but wrong (inaccurate). **(3 marks)**

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define (i) Dead Zone and (ii) Threshold of an instrument. How are they different from Resolution?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Threshold** is the *smallest input magnitude* that produces a *detectable* change in output from zero. Below threshold, the output is exactly zero.
- **Dead Zone** is the *total range* of input values over which the output does not change *at all*, even after the input crosses the threshold. It is caused by friction, backlash, and contact potentials.
