# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 marks)

### Question 1 — Definition + Salient Feature `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Q1.** *What is a function generator? List any four standard waveforms it produces.* (3 marks)

**Model Answer:**
A function generator is an electronic signal-source instrument that produces **standard periodic waveforms** of **adjustable frequency and amplitude** for testing, calibrating, and troubleshooting electronic circuits and sensors. The four standard waveforms are:
1. **Sine wave** — generated by a Wien-Bridge Oscillator.
2. **Square wave** — generated by an op-amp astable multivibrator (Schmitt trigger + RC).
3. **Triangular wave** — obtained by integrating the square wave via a Miller integrator.
4. **Sawtooth / ramp wave** — produced by an asymmetric charge-discharge current source.

---

### Question 2 — Salient Feature + Frequency-Sync `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Q2.** *Explain the "frequency-synchronous tuning" feature of a function generator with a neat block diagram.* (3 marks)

**Model Answer:**
The frequency-synchronous tuning feature ensures that **all four waveforms (sine, square, triangle, ramp) change frequency together** when the user rotates the single frequency-control knob. This is achieved by **mechanically ganging** the variable resistors (or capacitor banks) of:
- the Wien-bridge network of the sine oscillator,
- the timing RC of the Schmitt-trigger square-wave oscillator, and
- the integrating RC of the triangle generator,

so that a single dial movement changes *R* in **all three sub-oscillators** simultaneously, thereby producing a co-varying set of waveforms at the same frequency. A simplified block diagram:

```
          [Ganged R] ──► [Wien Bridge] ──► Sine
                       ──► [Schmitt + RC] ──► Square
                       ──► [Integrator]  ──► Triangle
```

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Wien-Bridge Sine Oscillator (Detailed) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]`

**(a) Derive the expression for the frequency of oscillation of a Wien-Bridge Oscillator using an op-amp. State the required gain and the value of the feedback factor at that frequency.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Step-by-Step, marks shown in brackets):**

[2 marks] **Step 1 — Draw/cite the Wien bridge network.** The series arm $Z_1 = R + 1/(j\omega C)$ and parallel arm $Z_2 = R \parallel (1/j\omega C) = R/(1+j\omega R C)$ form the positive-feedback network to the non-inverting terminal of an op-amp configured as a non-inverting amplifier with gain $A = 1 + R_f/R_g$.

[2 marks] **Step 2 — Apply the Barkhausen phase condition.** For sustained oscillation the loop phase must be zero. The zero-phase-shift condition of the Wien network is obtained when the *real part* of the denominator of $\beta = Z_2/(Z_1 + Z_2)$ becomes zero:

$$1 - \omega^2 R^2 C^2 = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{RC}$$

[2 marks] **Step 3 — Convert to Hz and state the feedback factor.** $f_0 = 1/(2\pi R C)$. Substituting $\omega_0 = 1/(RC)$ into $\beta$ yields $\beta = 1/3$.

[1 mark] **Step 4 — Required amplifier gain.** Barkhausen magnitude condition $A\beta = 1$ implies $A = 1/\beta = 3$, hence the feedback resistor ratio must be $R_f = 2R_g$.

$$\boxed{f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R C}, \quad \beta = \dfrac{1}{3}, \quad A = 3}$$

---

**(b) With reference to the design of a function generator, explain how sine, square and triangular waves are generated. Show how the ganged tuning element ensures synchronism.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

[2 marks] **Sine wave** — produced by a Wien-Bridge Oscillator (as in part (a)). $f = 1/(2\pi R C)$ with $\beta = 1/3$ and $A = 3$. An AGC loop (FET/thermistor/lamp in the feedback arm) keeps amplitude constant.

[2 marks] **Square wave** — produced by an op-amp astable multivibrator (Schmitt trigger) with a single RC timing branch. The capacitor charges between threshold voltages $\pm\beta V_{sat}$ where $\beta$ is the divider ratio. The frequency is $f = 1/(2RC\ln[(1+\beta)/(1-\beta)])$, which for the standard $\beta=1/3$ gives $f = 0.455/RC$.

[2 marks] **Triangular wave** — produced by feeding the square wave into a Miller integrator whose transfer is $V_{out} = -\dfrac{1}{R_{int}C_{int}}\int V_{in}\,dt$. The peak amplitude is $V_{tri,pk} = V_{sat}/(4 R_{int} C_{int} f)$.

[1 mark] **Synchronism** — A **ganged variable resistor (R)** is mechanically linked to all three sub-oscillators' timing RC. Because $f \propto 1/R$ in all three, varying $R$ changes the frequency of **all three waveforms identically** with a single dial — this is the *frequency-synchronous tuning* feature.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Astable Multivibrator + IC Function Generator (Alternative) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]`

**(a) With a circuit diagram, derive the expression for the time period of an op-amp astable multivibrator. Hence find the frequency for $\beta = 1/3$ and $R = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$, $C = 0.01\ \mu\text{F}$.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

[1 mark] **Diagram description:** A non-inverting Schmitt-trigger op-amp whose output saturates at $\pm V_{sat}$. The non-inverting input receives positive feedback through a divider $R_1, R_2$ with ratio $\beta = R_1/(R_1+R_2)$. The inverting input is tied to a capacitor $C$ that charges/discharges through $R$ toward $\pm V_{sat}$.

[2 marks] **Step 1 — Thresholds.** The voltage at the non-inverting terminal is $\beta V_{sat}$ in magnitude, so the Schmitt comparator switches when the capacitor voltage crosses $\pm \beta V_{sat}$:

$$V_{UT} = +\beta V_{sat}, \quad V_{LT} = -\beta V_{sat}$$

[2 marks] **Step 2 — Charging equation.** Assume $C$ is at $-V_{LT}$ and charging toward $+V_{sat}$ through $R$. After time $t$:

$$v_C(t) = V_{sat} - 2V_{sat} e^{-t/RC}$$

[1 mark] **Step 3 — Set $v_C(T/2) = +\beta V_{sat}$ and solve.** This gives $T = 2RC \ln[(1+\beta)/(1-\beta)]$.

[1 mark] **Step 4 — Numerical substitution** for $\beta = 1/3$, $R = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$, $C = 0.01\ \mu\text{F}$:

$$T = 2 \times 10^4 \times 10^{-8} \times \ln 2 = 2 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.693 = 1.386 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{s}$$

$$f = \dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{1}{1.386 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 7.22\ \text{kHz}$$

$$\boxed{f \approx 7.22\ \text{kHz}}$$

---

**(b) Describe the operation of the IC function generator (ICL8038 / XR-2206) with a functional block diagram. State any two advantages of using an IC over a discrete-component generator.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

[2 marks] **ICL8038 functional blocks:**
- A **current source** charges an external timing capacitor $C$ linearly.
- A **Schmitt trigger** flips when the capacitor voltage reaches an upper threshold, then the current source reverses and discharges $C$ until the lower threshold is hit.
- The capacitor voltage is the **triangular wave** (available at pin 3 of ICL8038).
- The Schmitt output is the **square wave** (pin 9).
- The triangle is fed to a **piecewise-linear sine shaper** — a network of diodes + resistors that progressively clip the triangle to approximate a sine wave (pin 2).

[1 mark] **Frequency-control equation:** $f = 0.33/(R C)$ (with $R$ controlling the constant current source).

[2 marks] **Frequency range:** 0.001 Hz to 300 kHz typical; amplitude up to $\sim V_{CC}/2$ peak.

[2 marks] **Advantages over discrete design:**
1. **Single-chip, smaller PCB** — eliminates hand-trimming of three separate RC pairs.
2. **Simultaneous, low-distortion** sine, square, triangle outputs with matched amplitude and phase.
3. **Easy frequency tuning** by varying a single external resistor — no need for a ganged mechanical potentiometer.
4. **Better thermal stability** (matched on-chip transistors).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks (and how to avoid it):**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in $T = 2RC\ln[(1+\beta)/(1-\beta)]$** — students write $T = RC\ln[\cdots]$ and lose 1 mark. Remember that the capacitor charges *twice* (from $-\beta V_{sat}$ to $+\beta V_{sat}$ and back), so the period is the **sum of two half-periods**.
> 2. **Confusing the feedback factor of the Wien bridge with that of the Schmitt trigger** — both are sometimes $\beta = 1/3$ in canonical designs, but they are *different* $\beta$'s in different networks. State the network explicitly.
> 3. **Not mentioning the AGC loop** when describing the sine-wave stage — KTU examiners allot 1 mark for amplitude-stability discussion. State that without AGC, the Wien bridge is unstable (amplitude either decays or clips).
> 4. **Writing $f = 1/RC$ instead of $f = 1/(2\pi R C)$** — losing the $2\pi$ factor is a classic 1-mark cut.
> 5. **Failing to draw the block diagram** for the IC function generator — at least a 2-mark cut. Always include triangle → Schmitt, Schmitt → square, triangle → sine-shaper interconnections.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Function generator** = signal source producing sine, square, triangle, and sawtooth waveforms with adjustable frequency and amplitude.
- **Sine wave** → **Wien-Bridge Oscillator** with $f_0 = 1/(2\pi RC)$, $\beta = 1/3$, $A = 3$, **AGC** mandatory.
- **Square wave** → **Op-amp astable multivibrator (Schmitt trigger + RC)** with $T = 2RC\ln[(1+\beta)/(1-\beta)]$.
- **For $\beta = 1/3$** → $T = 2RC\ln 2 = 1.386 RC$ → $f = 0.455/(RC)$.
- **Triangle wave** → **Miller integrator** of the square wave, with $V_{tri,pk} = V_{sat}/(4R_{int}C_{int}f)$.
- **Frequency-synchronous tuning** → a single **ganged R** tunes all three sub-oscillators simultaneously because $f \propto 1/R$ in each.
- **Standard output impedance** = **50 Ω**; **standard load** = 50 Ω; **$V_{out,loaded} = V_{OC}/2$** at full termination.
- **IC realisation** → **ICL8038** or **XR-2206**, with a built-in triangle oscillator, Schmitt trigger, and **sine shaper network** (diode-resistor piecewise-linear clipper).
- **Triangle amplitude falls with frequency** ($\propto 1/f$); commercial designs add an **ALC loop** to flatten amplitude across decades.
- **Output stage** typically includes a **push-pull buffer + step attenuator (0/20/40 dB)** to drive 50 Ω loads.
- **Phase relationship** in a real function generator: **square and triangle are in phase at zero crossings**; **sine leads square by 90°** when sourced from the Wien network.
- **Choice of waveform** is dictated by the test: **sine → linearity/THD, square → transient/slew, triangle → ADC linearity, sawtooth → CRO time-base**.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-mile exam tip (2024 scheme):** When the KTU question is worth 14 marks, always structure your answer as: (1) diagram (2 marks) → (2) equation/derivation (5 marks) → (3) substitution/numerical (2 marks) → (4) discussion/real-world (3 marks) → (5) conclusion (2 marks). The above model answers follow this exact template.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bridges: Measurement of Resistance: Wheatstone's Bridge
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT721 - SENSORS AND INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Bridges: Measurement of Resistance: Wheatstone's Bridge

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Wheatstone Bridge** is a null-type, deflection-based electrical network consisting of four resistive arms arranged in a diamond (bridge) configuration, with a sensitive **galvanometer** connected across one diagonal (the detector diagonal) and a **DC excitation source** connected across the other diagonal (the source diagonal). It is the classical, most fundamental instrument used for the precise measurement of an **unknown electrical resistance** by comparison against three known reference resistances.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (OEERT721 - Module 3):**
> The Wheatstone Bridge is the foundational DC bridge circuit. Mastery of its balance condition, sensitivity, and error sources is **mandatory** as it is a direct prerequisite for understanding more complex bridges (Kelvin, Maxwell, Anderson, Schering, Wien) covered later in the module.

The four arms of the bridge are conventionally labeled as:
- $R_1$ and $R_2$ — the two ratio arms (often a precision decade resistor box)
- $R_3$ — the standard (known) arm (precision reference)
- $R_4$ (or $R_x$) — the unknown resistance to be measured

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Mechanical Balance Scale

Imagine a perfectly balanced two-pan weighing scale:

- The **battery (V)** is the gravitational pull — it constantly tries to push current through both branches of the circuit.
- The **four resistors** ($R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4$) are the loads on the two pans of the scale. The current splits and flows through the two parallel branches: (A→B→D) and (A→C→D).
- The **galvanometer (G)** is the **pointer needle** of the scale — it shows whether the two "pans" (nodes B and C) are at the same potential.

When the scale is perfectly balanced, the needle stays at zero — meaning **no current flows through the galvanometer**. This is the famous **"null condition"** or **balance condition**. Just as you deduce an unknown weight by placing known weights on the opposite pan, you deduce an unknown resistance by adjusting known resistances until the galvanometer reads zero.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Term — Null Method:** A measurement technique where the indicator (galvanometer) is brought to zero deflection. The advantage is that the reading is **independent** of the accuracy of the galvanometer scale, the supply voltage, or the internal resistance of the detector.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard / Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Measurement Range | **1 Ω to 1 MΩ** (typical) | Medium resistance range |
| Best Accuracy | **±0.1%** (with precision components) | Laboratory-grade instruments |
| Excitation Source | **DC Voltage** (1.5 V – 10 V typical) | Stable low-voltage source required |
| Detector | **D'Arsonval Galvanometer** | Sensitive to microampere-level currents |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Circuit Topology and Operating Principle

The Wheatstone Bridge circuit is constructed as follows:

1. **Nodes A, B, C, D** form a closed diamond loop (a quadrilateral).
2. **Arm AB** contains resistor $R_1$.
3. **Arm BC** contains the galvanometer $G$ (with internal resistance $R_g$).
4. **Arm AD** contains resistor $R_2$.
5. **Arm DC** contains the unknown resistor $R_4$.
6. **Arm AC** contains the DC excitation source $V$ with negligible internal resistance.
7. **Arm BD** would conceptually be the galvanometer diagonal — this is the "detector diagonal."

When a DC voltage $V$ is applied between nodes A and C, currents $I_1$ and $I_2$ flow through the two branches:

- **Branch 1 (A → B → C):** Carries current $I_1$ through $R_1$ and then through $R_3$ back to C.
- **Branch 2 (A → D → C):** Carries current $I_2$ through $R_2$ and then through $R_4$ back to C.
- **Galvanometer current** $I_g$ flows from B to D (or vice versa) depending on the potential difference $V_B - V_D$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Bridge"?** The galvanometer "bridges" nodes B and D across the two parallel branches — it acts as a bridge between them, sensing any potential imbalance.

## 2.2 The Balance Condition — The Heart of the Wheatstone Bridge

The bridge is said to be **balanced** when the galvanometer current $I_g = 0$. When $I_g = 0$:

- The potentials at nodes B and D are **equal**: $V_B = V_D$.
- No current is diverted through the galvanometer branch.
- All current flowing through $R_1$ also flows through $R_3$ (same branch).
- All current flowing through $R_2$ also flows through $R_4$ (same branch).

This gives us the celebrated **balance equation**:

$$R_4 = R_3 \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1}$$

This is the most critical formula in this entire module.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R_x = R_3 \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1}$ | Balance condition (most important) | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| 2 | $V_B - V_D = V \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1+R_2} - V \cdot \dfrac{R_4}{R_3+R_4}$ | Off-balance detector voltage | Volts (V) |
| 3 | $I_g = \dfrac{V \cdot (R_2 R_3 - R_1 R_4)}{R_g (R_1+R_2)(R_3+R_4) + R_1 R_2 (R_3+R_4) + R_3 R_4 (R_1+R_2)}$ | Galvanometer current (general case) | Amperes (A) |
| 4 | $S_v = \dfrac{V}{(R_1+R_2)(R_3+R_4)}$ | Bridge voltage sensitivity (with matched source/galvanometer) | V/$\Omega$ |
| 5 | $R_1 R_4 = R_2 R_3$ | Product balance equation (alternate form) | $\Omega^2$ |
| 6 | $\delta R_x = R_3 \cdot \dfrac{\delta R_2}{R_1} - R_3 \cdot \dfrac{R_2 \cdot \delta R_1}{R_1^2} + \dfrac{R_2}{R_1} \cdot \delta R_3$ | Error propagation in $R_x$ | $\Omega$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Point:** Formula (3) — the general galvanometer current equation — is the starting point for **all bridge sensitivity derivations** that the examiner loves to test. Memorize its structure (it is symmetric in swapping index pairs).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Precision Resistance Measurement:** Used in laboratories and calibration centers to measure standard resistors, wire-wound resistors, and resistor networks.
- **Sensor Transducer Interfacing:** Forms the core signal-conditioning circuit for **strain gauges**, **RTDs (Resistance Temperature Detectors)**, **thermistors**, and **LVDTs** (when used with appropriate excitation).
- **Wheatstone Bridge ICs:** Modern ASICs (e.g., INA125, AD7799) integrate precision matched resistors and instrumentation amplifiers specifically to support bridge-based sensors.
- **Educational Demonstrator:** The classic introductory circuit taught in every instrumentation course worldwide.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Balance Condition $R_x = R_3 \cdot (R_2 / R_1)$

**Step 1: Define the currents in each branch.**

Let $I_1$ be the current flowing through the upper branch (A → B → C) and $I_2$ be the current flowing through the lower branch (A → D → C). The galvanometer current is $I_g$, flowing from B to D.

**Step 2: Apply Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) at nodes B and D.**

At node B:
$$I_1 = I_3 + I_g$$
where $I_3$ is the current through $R_3$ continuing to node C.

At node D:
$$I_2 + I_g = I_4$$
where $I_4$ is the current through $R_4$ continuing to node C.

**Step 3: Apply the balance assumption $I_g = 0$.**

When the bridge is balanced, $I_g = 0$. Therefore:

$$I_1 = I_3 \quad \text{and} \quad I_2 = I_4$$

This is the **critical simplification** — at null condition, no current branches off into the galvanometer.

**Step 4: Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) to the two branches.**

For the upper branch (A → B → C), going around the loop:
$$V = I_1 R_1 + I_1 R_3$$

For the lower branch (A → D → C), going around the loop:
$$V = I_2 R_2 + I_2 R_4$$

**Step 5: Express the potential at node B and node D with respect to node A (ground reference at A, i.e., $V_A = 0$).**

Potential at B (using voltage divider on the upper branch):
$$V_B = V - I_1 R_1 = I_1 R_3$$

Potential at D (using voltage divider on the lower branch):
$$V_D = V - I_2 R_2 = I_2 R_4$$

**Step 6: Apply the null condition $V_B = V_D$ (since $I_g = 0$ means no potential difference across the galvanometer).**

$$I_1 R_3 = I_2 R_4$$

**Step 7: Equate the branch currents using the supply voltage equation.**

From Step 4:

$$I_1 = \frac{V}{R_1 + R_3} \quad \text{and} \quad I_2 = \frac{V}{R_2 + R_4}$$

**Step 8: Substitute into the balance equation $I_1 R_3 = I_2 R_4$.**

$$\frac{V \cdot R_3}{R_1 + R_3} = \frac{V \cdot R_4}{R_2 + R_4}$$

**Step 9: Cross-multiply and simplify.**

$$R_3 (R_2 + R_4) = R_4 (R_1 + R_3)$$

$$R_2 R_3 + R_3 R_4 = R_1 R_4 + R_3 R_4$$

**Step 10: Cancel the common term $R_3 R_4$ from both sides.**

$$R_2 R_3 = R_1 R_4$$

**Step 11: Solve for the unknown $R_4$ (or $R_x$).**

$$\boxed{R_x = R_4 = R_3 \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1}}$$

This is the **fundamental balance equation of the Wheatstone Bridge**. ✔

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Key Insights from the Derivation

| Insight | Implication |
|---|---|
| Balance depends only on **ratios** of resistances | Absolute values of $R_1, R_2, R_3$ do not matter — only their ratios |
| Supply voltage $V$ cancels out | The null reading is **independent** of supply voltage stability |
| Galvanometer resistance $R_g$ does not appear in the balance equation | The null condition is independent of galvanometer characteristics |
| $R_1 = R_2$ (ratio arms equal) ⇒ $R_x = R_3$ | Symmetric "equal-ratio" bridge — simplest practical implementation |

## 3.3 Sensitivity of the Wheatstone Bridge

**Sensitivity** is defined as the change in galvanometer current per unit change in the unknown resistance, evaluated near balance:

$$S_I = \left. \frac{\partial I_g}{\partial R_4} \right|_{\text{balance}}$$

Starting from the general galvanometer current equation and differentiating with respect to $R_4$ at the balance point, the standard result (for a bridge where $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R$ and $R_g$ is matched) simplifies to:

$$S_I = \frac{V}{4R(2R + R_g)}$$

The **voltage sensitivity** (change in detector voltage per unit $\Delta R$ at balance) is:

$$S_V = \frac{V}{4R}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Rule:** To maximize sensitivity, (1) increase the supply voltage $V$, (2) use a galvanometer with low internal resistance $R_g$, and (3) keep the bridge arm resistances as small as practical. However, increasing $V$ too much causes **self-heating** of the resistors, which changes their values.

## 3.4 Sources of Error in Wheatstone Bridge Measurements

1. **Lead Resistance Error:** Particularly significant for low resistances ($< 1\ \Omega$). The resistance of connecting wires and solder joints adds to the unknown. Solution: Use **Kelvin Double Bridge**.
2. **Contact Resistance Error:** Dirty or oxidized switch contacts in decade resistance boxes introduce unknown series resistance. Solution: Use clean, tight, gold-plated contacts.
3. **Self-Heating Error:** Power dissipation $I^2 R$ in the arms raises the resistor temperature, changing the resistance value. Solution: Use pulsed excitation or low current.
4. **Thermoelectric EMF Error:** At junctions of dissimilar metals, small EMFs (microvolts) are generated, producing spurious galvanometer deflection. Solution: Use **false-null** technique or reverse the supply and average.
5. **Galvanometer Sensitivity Limit:** If the galvanometer is not sensitive enough, the "null" may not be exactly at balance but at a small residual deflection. Solution: Use a more sensitive galvanometer or a chopper-stabilized detector.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Wheatstone Bridge — Circuit Topology (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["Node A: Positive Supply"]
    nodeB["Node B: Galvanometer Junction Top"]
    nodeC["Node C: Negative Supply / Ground"]
    nodeD["Node D: Galvanometer Junction Bottom"]
    R1["R1: Ratio Arm 1"]
    R2["R2: Ratio Arm 2"]
    R3["R3: Standard Resistor"]
    R4["R4: Unknown Resistance Rx"]
    Vsrc["V: DC Excitation Source"]
    GALV["G: Galvanometer Detector"]
    nodeA --- R1
    R1 --- nodeB
    nodeB --- R3
    R3 --- nodeC
    nodeA --- R2
    R2 --- nodeD
    nodeD --- R4
    R4 --- nodeC
    nodeB --- GALV
    GALV --- nodeD
    Vsrc --- nodeA
    Vsrc --- nodeC
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Architecture — Measurement Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    SRC["DC Excitation Source V"] --> BRIDGE["Wheatstone Bridge Network"]
    BRIDGE --> SENS["Strain / Temp / Resistance Change in Rx"]
    SENS --> DEBAL["Imbalance Voltage VBD"]
    DEBAL --> DET["Galvanometer / Amplifier Detector"]
    DET --> READ["Display: Null or Deflection"]
    READ --> COMP["Compare with Reference Arms"]
    COMP --> CALC["Calculate Rx using Balance Equation"]
    CALC --> OUT["Measured Resistance Value"]
```

## 4.3 Bridge Balance Concept — Visual Logic

```mermaid
graph TD
    APPLY["Apply DC Voltage V across A-C diagonal"] --> SPLIT["Current Splits into Two Branches"]
    SPLIT --> BR1["Branch 1: I1 through R1 and R3"]
    SPLIT --> BR2["Branch 2: I2 through R2 and R4"]
    BR1 --> VB["Potential at Node B: VB = V x R3 divided by R1+R3"]
    BR2 --> VD["Potential at Node D: VD = V x R4 divided by R2+R4"]
    VB --> COMP["Compare VB and VD"]
    VD --> COMP
    COMP --> DECIDE{"Is VB equal to VD?"}
    DECIDE --> YES["YES: Null Condition. Ig equals 0. Bridge Balanced."]
    DECIDE --> NO["NO: Galvanometer Deflects. Adjust R3 until Null."]
    NO --> ADJUST["Iterate: Change R3 or R2/R1 ratio"]
    ADJUST --> DECIDE
    YES --> RESULT["Read R3 and Ratio Arms. Compute Rx equals R3 times R2 over R1."]
```

## 4.4 Key Takeaways from the Diagrams

- The **excitation diagonal (A–C)** carries the total supply current, while the **detector diagonal (B–D)** ideally carries zero current at null.
- The bridge is essentially **two voltage dividers in parallel**, and null is achieved when the divider outputs are equal.
- The functional flow shows that the **bridge is a comparator**, not a direct-reading instrument — it compares the unknown to known references.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: Define a Wheatstone Bridge. State the balance condition and write the expression for the unknown resistance.**

**Model Answer:**

A Wheatstone Bridge is a four-arm null-type electrical network used for the accurate measurement of an unknown resistance. The four resistive arms $R_1$, $R_2$, $R_3$, and $R_4$ are arranged in a diamond configuration, with a DC excitation source connected across one diagonal and a sensitive galvanometer across the other diagonal.

The **balance condition** is the state at which the galvanometer current $I_g = 0$, indicating that the potentials at the two detector nodes are equal. Mathematically:

$$R_1 R_4 = R_2 R_3$$

The unknown resistance is therefore:

$$R_x = R_4 = R_3 \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1}$$

This is the classical null method of resistance measurement. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: Why is the Wheatstone Bridge classified as a "null-type" instrument? List any two advantages of the null method.**

**Model Answer:**

The Wheatstone Bridge is called a **null-type instrument** because the measurement is performed by adjusting the known arms until the detector (galvanometer) reads exactly zero — i.e., the "null" condition. The unknown resistance is then computed from the settings of the known arms, not from the magnitude of the detector deflection.

**Two advantages of the null method:**

1. **Independence from supply voltage stability:** As seen in the balance equation, the source voltage $V$ cancels out. Thus, fluctuations in the battery/excitation voltage do not affect the null reading.
2. **Independence from galvanometer calibration:** Since the detector is used only as a null indicator (zero or non-zero), the absolute accuracy of the galvanometer scale does not matter. Only its **sensitivity** (ability to detect a small imbalance) is required.

**(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question Choice A [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**Q: With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a Wheatstone Bridge. Derive the balance condition and discuss the sources of error.**

#### Part (a) — Working Principle and Circuit Diagram (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

The Wheatstone Bridge consists of four resistive arms $R_1$, $R_2$, $R_3$, and $R_4$ forming a closed quadrilateral. A DC voltage source $V$ is connected across nodes A and C (the supply diagonal), and a galvanometer $G$ is connected across nodes B and D (the detector diagonal).

When the supply is switched on, currents $I_1$ and $I_2$ flow through the two branches. The galvanometer indicates the **potential difference** $V_B - V_D$. If $V_B \neq V_D$, a current $I_g$ flows through the galvanometer, producing a visible deflection.

The procedure to measure an unknown $R_x = R_4$:

1. Place the unknown resistance in arm AD.
2. Place a known variable standard resistor $R_3$ in arm DC.
3. Use a decade resistance box to form the ratio arms $R_1$ and $R_2$.
4. Adjust $R_3$ (and/or the ratio $R_2/R_1$) until the galvanometer reads exactly zero.
5. At this null, substitute the values into the balance equation to compute $R_x$.

**Circuit Diagram (describe for exam):**

Draw the classic diamond shape: top node B and bottom node D, with R1 on the upper-left, R3 on the upper-right, R2 on the lower-left, and R4 on the lower-right. The battery V connects to the left and right nodes (A and C); the galvanometer connects between the top and bottom nodes (B and D). Label all four arm currents and the galvanometer current $I_g$.

#### Part (b) — Derivation of Balance Condition and Sources of Error (7 marks)

**Derivation:**

At balance, $I_g = 0$, so all current in the upper branch flows through both $R_1$ and $R_3$. Applying KVL to the upper branch:

$$V = I_1 (R_1 + R_3) \Rightarrow I_1 = \frac{V}{R_1 + R_3}$$

Similarly, for the lower branch:

$$V = I_2 (R_2 + R_4) \Rightarrow I_2 = \frac{V}{R_2 + R_4}$$

Since no current flows through the galvanometer, the potential drop across $R_3$ must equal the potential drop across $R_4$ (because $V_B = V_D$):

$$I_1 R_3 = I_2 R_4$$

Substituting the expressions for $I_1$ and $I_2$:

$$\frac{V R_3}{R_1 + R_3} = \frac{V R_4}{R_2 + R_4}$$

After cross-multiplication and simplification:

$$R_2 R_3 = R_1 R_4 \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_x = R_3 \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1}$$

**Sources of Error:**

1. **Resistance of leads and contacts** — adds in series with $R_x$.
2. **Heating effect** — $I^2 R$ power dissipation changes the resistance.
3. **Thermoelectric EMFs** at junctions produce spurious galvanometer readings.
4. **Limited galvanometer sensitivity** — may not detect very small imbalances.
5. **Inaccuracy of the standard resistor and ratio arms** — directly multiplies into the result.

**(14 marks total)**

---

### Question Choice B [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**Q: Derive the expression for the galvanometer current in an unbalanced Wheatstone Bridge. Define bridge sensitivity and explain the factors affecting it.**

#### Part (a) — Galvanometer Current Derivation (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

Consider the unbalanced bridge with supply voltage $V$ across A–C. The four arms are $R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4$ and the galvanometer has internal resistance $R_g$.

**Thevenin Equivalent Method (standard approach taught in KTU):**

**Step 1:** Remove the galvanometer branch (B–D open). The voltage across the open detector diagonal is:

$$V_{th} = V_B - V_D = V \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} - V \cdot \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4}$$

$$\boxed{V_{th} = V \cdot \frac{R_2 R_3 - R_1 R_4}{(R_1 + R_2)(R_3 + R_4)}}$$

**Step 2:** Find the Thevenin resistance seen from B–D (with the voltage source short-circuited):

$$R_{th} = \frac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2} + \frac{R_3 R_4}{R_3 + R_4}$$

**Step 3:** The galvanometer current is then:

$$I_g = \frac{V_{th}}{R_g + R_{th}}$$

Substituting and simplifying:

$$I_g = \frac{V (R_2 R_3 - R_1 R_4)}{R_g (R_1 + R_2)(R_3 + R_4) + R_1 R_2 (R_3 + R_4) + R_3 R_4 (R_1 + R_2)}$$

At balance, $R_2 R_3 = R_1 R_4$, so the numerator becomes zero and $I_g = 0$ as expected. ✔

#### Part (b) — Bridge Sensitivity and Affecting Factors (7 marks)

**Definition:**

**Bridge sensitivity** $S$ is defined as the rate of change of the output (galvanometer current or detector voltage) per unit change in the measurand (unknown resistance), evaluated at or near the balance condition.

**Current Sensitivity:**

$$S_I = \left. \frac{\partial I_g}{\partial R_4} \right|_{R_4 = R_x} = \frac{V}{(R_1 + R_2)(R_3 + R_4) + 2 R_g (R_1 + R_2 + R_3 + R_4)}$$

**Voltage Sensitivity:**

$$S_V = \left. \frac{\partial V_{th}}{\partial R_4} \right|_{\text{balance}} = \frac{V}{(R_1 + R_2)(R_3 + R_4)}$$

**Factors Affecting Sensitivity:**

1. **Excitation Voltage $V$:** Sensitivity is directly proportional to $V$. Doubling $V$ doubles $S$. (Limited by self-heating.)
2. **Galvanometer Resistance $R_g$:** Lower $R_g$ gives higher $S_I$. For maximum current sensitivity, match $R_g$ to the Thevenin resistance.
3. **Magnitude of Bridge Arms:** Smaller arm resistances yield higher sensitivity, but reduce the measurable range.
4. **Symmetry of the Bridge:** Maximum sensitivity occurs when all four arms are equal (i.e., $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R$), and the galvanometer is matched to the source impedance.
5. **Type of Detector:** A chopper-stabilized amplifier or electronic null detector gives far higher practical sensitivity than a passive D'Arsonval galvanometer.

**(14 marks total)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing $R_g$ dependence:** A common mistake is to write the balance condition as dependent on $R_g$. It is **NOT** — balance is independent of $R_g$. $R_g$ only affects sensitivity.
> 2. **Skipping the Thevenin step:** When deriving the unbalanced galvanometer current, students often skip the Thevenin equivalent and try direct mesh analysis. This works but is error-prone. KTU examiners expect the Thevenin method.
> 3. **Missing the "null method advantages":** Simply writing the balance equation is not enough for full marks. Always add the **two or three key advantages** of the null method (independence from $V$ and $R_g$).
> 4. **Units and notation:** Always explicitly write "$R_x = R_3 \cdot (R_2 / R_1)$ in Ohms" and clearly state which arm is the unknown.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the KTU Exam:**

- ✅ The Wheatstone Bridge is a **null-type** resistance measurement instrument with **four resistive arms**.
- ✅ The **balance condition** is $R_1 R_4 = R_2 R_3$, giving the unknown as $R_x = R_3 \cdot (R_2 / R_1)$.
- ✅ At balance: $I_g = 0$, $V_B = V_D$, and the supply voltage $V$ cancels out from the equation.
- ✅ The balance condition is **independent** of the supply voltage $V$ and the galvanometer resistance $R_g$.
- ✅ The galvanometer is connected across the **detector diagonal** (B–D); the battery is across the **source diagonal** (A–C).
- ✅ The bridge measures **medium resistances** (1 Ω to 1 MΩ). For very low resistances, use the **Kelvin Bridge**. For very high resistances, use a **Megger** or **Wheatstone-Megger combination**.
- ✅ The **sensitivity** $S$ is proportional to supply voltage $V$ and inversely related to bridge arm magnitudes.
- ✅ **Key sources of error:** lead resistance, contact resistance, self-heating ($I^2 R$), thermoelectric EMFs, and limited galvanometer sensitivity.
- ✅ **Practical applications:** strain gauge signal conditioning, RTD-based temperature measurement, thermistor interfacing, precision laboratory resistance standards.
- ✅ **Modern relevance:** The Wheatstone Bridge principle is the foundation of nearly all **bridge-type sensor interfaces** in industrial IoT and process instrumentation.
- ✅ For maximum sensitivity, all four arms should be **equal** ($R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R_4$) and the galvanometer should be **impedance-matched** to the bridge.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: AC Bridges - Capacitance and Inductance Comparison Bridge
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT721 - SENSORS AND INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** AC Bridges - Capacitance and Inductance Comparison Bridge

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **AC Bridge (General Definition):** An AC bridge is a null-deflection type electrical network, derived from the Wheatstone DC bridge, used for the precise measurement of electrical quantities such as resistance, inductance, capacitance, impedance, and frequency. It operates on the principle of balancing two parallel branches fed by an AC source, with detection achieved using a sensitive AC detector (headphones, vibration galvanometer, or tuned amplifier).

An AC bridge has four arms (impedances $\mathbf{Z_1, Z_2, Z_3, Z_4}$), an AC excitation source (sinusoidal, typically **1 kHz** or **50 Hz** mains), and a null detector. The **general balance condition** requires:

$$Z_1 \cdot Z_4 = Z_2 \cdot Z_3$$

> [!NOTE]
> Unlike a DC bridge (which has only one magnitude balance condition), an AC bridge requires **two simultaneous balance conditions** — one for magnitude and one for phase angle — making it inherently more complex but vastly more versatile.

## 1.2 Capacitance Comparison Bridge (De Sauty's Bridge)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Capacitance Comparison Bridge:** A specific AC bridge configuration used to measure an unknown capacitance by directly comparing it against a known standard capacitance. The classical form is **De Sauty's Bridge**, suitable only for *lossless* (ideal) capacitors. The lossy capacitor variant is **Wien's Bridge**.

## 1.3 Inductance Comparison Bridge (Maxwell's Inductance Bridge)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inductance Comparison Bridge:** A specific AC bridge used to measure an unknown inductance. The most common implementation is **Maxwell's Inductance-Capacitance Bridge**, which compares an unknown inductive impedance against a known standard *capacitor*. This converts an inductance measurement into a capacitance measurement, taking advantage of the high stability of standard capacitors.

## 1.4 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — The Mechanical Balance Scale:**
> Imagine a classic two-pan balance scale. You place an unknown weight on the left pan, and you keep adding **known** standard weights on the right pan until the horizontal pointer is perfectly centered (null deflection). The unknown weight is then equal to the sum of the standards.
>
> An AC bridge works on the *exact same principle*, but instead of weights, it balances **electrical impedances**. The detector pointer corresponds to the "tilt indicator" of the scale. The two simultaneous balance conditions (magnitude AND phase) are like balancing a scale that is allowed to tilt in *two* independent directions (front-back and left-right) — both must be zeroed simultaneously for true equilibrium.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Frequencies Used in KTU Labs:**
> - **Power frequency bridge:** $f = 50$ **Hz** (India mains)
> - Audio frequency bridge: $f = 1$ **kHz** (most common in laboratory work)
> - High-frequency bridge: $f = 10$–$100$ **kHz** (precision work)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 General Theory of AC Bridge Balance

Consider a generalized four-arm AC bridge with impedances $\mathbf{Z_1, Z_2, Z_3, Z_4}$ arranged in a ring. An AC source is connected between nodes **A** (top) and **C** (bottom), and a null detector between nodes **B** (left) and **D** (right).

- Let $\mathbf{I_1}$ flow through arm AB (impedance $Z_1$)
- Let $\mathbf{I_2}$ flow through arm AD (impedance $Z_2$)
- The detector current must equal **zero** at balance

The current divider logic gives the balance condition as:

$$Z_1 \cdot Z_4 = Z_2 \cdot Z_3 \quad \text{(in complex form)}$$

In polar form, with $Z_k = \vert Z_k \vert \angle \theta_k$:

$$\vert Z_1 \vert \cdot \vert Z_4 \vert = \vert Z_2 \vert \cdot \vert Z_3 \vert \quad \text{(magnitude balance)}$$

$$\angle Z_1 + \angle Z_4 = \angle Z_2 + \angle Z_3 \quad \text{(phase balance)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why two conditions?** Because a complex number has *two* degrees of freedom. Balancing an AC bridge is equivalent to solving a system of two real equations for two unknown real parameters (e.g., unknown R and L of a coil).

## 2.2 Why Use AC Bridges?

- DC bridges (Wheatstone) **cannot** measure pure inductance or capacitance because their reactance at DC ($\omega = 0$) is zero — they become indistinguishable from a short or open circuit.
- AC excitation provides a non-zero frequency $\omega = 2\pi f$, giving inductive reactance $X_L = \omega L$ and capacitive reactance $X_C = 1/(\omega C)$ a finite, measurable value.
- This makes AC bridges indispensable for testing reactive components.

## 2.3 Key Components of an AC Bridge

| Component | Function | Typical Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **AC Source** | Provides sinusoidal excitation | Oscillator at **1 kHz** |
| **Detector** | Indicates null condition | Headphones, vibration galvanometer, tuned amplifier, oscilloscope |
| **Standard Impedances** | Known reference elements | Standard resistor $\pm 0.1\%$, standard capacitor $\pm 0.1\%$ |
| **Unknown Impedance** | Component under test | Unknown R, L, C, or Z |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize this table — it forms the backbone of any AC bridge problem in KTU exams.**

| Bridge Type | Unknown Element | Balance Equation 1 (R) | Balance Equation 2 (L or C) | Phasor Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **De Sauty's** (Capacitance Comparison) | $C_1$ | Not applicable (lossless) | $C_1 = \left(\dfrac{R_4}{R_3}\right) \cdot C_2$ | Purely reactive arms |
| **Wien's** (Lossy Capacitor) | $C_1$ with $r_1$ | $r_1 = \dfrac{R_4}{R_3} \cdot r_2 - R_1$ | $C_1 = \left(\dfrac{R_3}{R_4}\right) \cdot \dfrac{1}{\omega^2 C_2^2 \cdot r_2}$ | Frequency dependent |
| **Maxwell's LC** (Inductance Comparison) | $L_1$ with $r_1$ | $r_1 = \dfrac{R_2 \cdot R_3}{R_1}$ | $L_1 = R_2 \cdot R_3 \cdot C_1$ | $\omega$ cancels out |
| **Hay's** (High Q Inductor) | $L_1$ with $r_1$ | $r_1 = \dfrac{\omega^2 R_1 R_2 R_3 C_1^2}{1 + \omega^2 R_1^2 C_1^2}$ | $L_1 = \dfrac{R_2 R_3 C_1}{1 + \omega^2 R_1^2 C_1^2}$ | Frequency dependent |

**Standard Formula Conventions Used Throughout KTU Textbooks:**

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Resistance | $R$ | **Ohm ($\Omega$)** | Purely real |
| Inductance | $L$ | **Henry (H)** | Often in **mH** or **$\mu$H** |
| Capacitance | $C$ | **Farad (F)** | Often in **$\mu$F, nF, pF** |
| Angular frequency | $\omega = 2\pi f$ | **rad/s** | At $f = 1$ **kHz**, $\omega = 6283$ **rad/s** |
| Inductive reactance | $X_L = \omega L$ | **Ohm ($\Omega$)** | Positive imaginary |
| Capacitive reactance | $X_C = 1/(\omega C)$ | **Ohm ($\Omega$)** | Negative imaginary |
| Q factor of coil | $Q = \omega L / r$ | **Dimensionless** | Indicates coil "lossiness" |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Calibration laboratories:** National Physical Laboratory (NPL), India uses AC bridges as primary standards.
- **Transformer manufacturer:** Quality control of winding inductance and leakage reactance.
- **Cable industry:** Measurement of cable capacitance per km to characterize insulation.
- **Filter design:** Verification of L and C values in tuned circuits before PCB assembly.
- **Sensor signal conditioning:** Many transducers (LVDT, capacitive pressure sensor) are read using AC bridge techniques.

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: De Sauty's Capacitance Comparison Bridge

### 3.1.1 Circuit Description

De Sauty's bridge measures an unknown lossless capacitor $C_1$ by comparing it with a known standard capacitor $C_2$. The two remaining arms are non-inductive (purely resistive) resistors $R_3$ and $R_4$.

**Arm arrangement:**
- **Arm 1 (AB):** Unknown capacitor $C_1$ — impedance $Z_1 = \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_1} = -\dfrac{j}{\omega C_1}$
- **Arm 2 (AD):** Standard capacitor $C_2$ — impedance $Z_2 = \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_2} = -\dfrac{j}{\omega C_2}$
- **Arm 3 (BC):** Pure resistor $R_3$ — impedance $Z_3 = R_3$
- **Arm 4 (DC):** Pure resistor $R_4$ — impedance $Z_4 = R_4$

**Source:** AC source between nodes A and C.
**Detector:** Null detector between nodes B and D.

### 3.1.2 Applying the General Balance Condition

At null (zero detector current), the standard AC bridge balance condition requires:

$$Z_1 \cdot Z_4 = Z_2 \cdot Z_3$$

Substituting the four arm impedances:

$$\left(-\dfrac{j}{\omega C_1}\right) \cdot R_4 = \left(-\dfrac{j}{\omega C_2}\right) \cdot R_3$$

### 3.1.3 Algebraic Simplification

The factor $\left(-\dfrac{j}{\omega}\right)$ appears on **both sides** of the equation. Dividing both sides by this common factor:

$$\dfrac{R_4}{C_1} = \dfrac{R_3}{C_2}$$

Cross-multiplying:

$$R_4 \cdot C_2 = R_3 \cdot C_1$$

Solving for the unknown capacitance $C_1$:

$$C_1 = \left(\dfrac{R_4}{R_3}\right) \cdot C_2$$

This is the **balance equation** for De Sauty's capacitance comparison bridge.

### 3.1.4 Phasor Analysis (Phase Balance)

In polar form:
- Arm 1: $Z_1 = \dfrac{1}{\omega C_1} \angle -90°$
- Arm 2: $Z_2 = \dfrac{1}{\omega C_2} \angle -90°$
- Arm 3: $Z_3 = R_3 \angle 0°$
- Arm 4: $Z_4 = R_4 \angle 0°$

Phase balance check:
$$\angle Z_1 + \angle Z_4 = -90° + 0° = -90°$$
$$\angle Z_2 + \angle Z_3 = -90° + 0° = -90°$$

Phase condition **automatically satisfied** (both sides equal $-90°$), which is consistent with the use of two pure capacitors and two pure resistors. The capacitor voltage **lags** the current by $90°$, but the resistors introduce no phase shift.

### 3.1.5 Limitation of De Sauty's Bridge

> [!WARNING]
> De Sauty's bridge is valid **only for lossless (ideal) capacitors**. A real capacitor has a small series resistance (or equivalently, a parallel leakage resistance) due to the dielectric loss. If we use De Sauty's bridge on a lossy capacitor, the balance equation gives a value of $C_1$ that is frequency-dependent and physically meaningless.

For lossy capacitors, the **Wien Bridge** (modified De Sauty) must be used.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Wien Bridge for Lossy Capacitors (Capacitance Comparison Extension)

### 3.2.1 Circuit Description

In practice, every capacitor has a small equivalent series resistance (ESR) $r_1$ in series with the ideal capacitance $C_1$. Wien's bridge handles this:

- **Arm 1 (AB):** Unknown lossy capacitor — modeled as $C_1$ in series with $r_1$
  $$Z_1 = r_1 + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_1} = r_1 - \dfrac{j}{\omega C_1}$$
- **Arm 2 (AD):** Standard lossy capacitor — $C_2$ in series with $r_2$
  $$Z_2 = r_2 - \dfrac{j}{\omega C_2}$$
- **Arm 3 (BC):** Pure resistor $R_3$
- **Arm 4 (DC):** Pure resistor $R_4$

### 3.2.2 Applying the Balance Condition

$$Z_1 \cdot Z_4 = Z_2 \cdot Z_3$$

$$\left(r_1 - \dfrac{j}{\omega C_1}\right) \cdot R_4 = \left(r_2 - \dfrac{j}{\omega C_2}\right) \cdot R_3$$

Expanding both sides:

$$R_4 \cdot r_1 - j\dfrac{R_4}{\omega C_1} = R_3 \cdot r_2 - j\dfrac{R_3}{\omega C_2}$$

### 3.2.3 Separating Real and Imaginary Parts

By the **principle of complex equation equality**, the real and imaginary parts must independently match.

**Real part equation:**
$$R_4 \cdot r_1 = R_3 \cdot r_2$$
$$\therefore r_1 = \left(\dfrac{R_3}{R_4}\right) \cdot r_2$$

**Imaginary part equation:**
$$\dfrac{R_4}{\omega C_1} = \dfrac{R_3}{\omega C_2}$$
$$\dfrac{R_4}{C_1} = \dfrac{R_3}{C_2}$$
$$\therefore C_1 = \left(\dfrac{R_4}{R_3}\right) \cdot C_2$$

> [!NOTE]
> The capacitance equation has the **same form** as De Sauty's bridge, but now includes the series resistance $r_1$ as an *additional* measured quantity. The bridge still depends on the known $r_2$ of the standard.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Maxwell's Inductance-Capacitance Bridge (Inductance Comparison)

### 3.3.1 Circuit Description

Maxwell's bridge measures an unknown inductive coil (modeled as $L_1$ in series with $r_1$) by comparing it with a known standard capacitor. This is the most widely used "inductance comparison" technique.

- **Arm 1 (AB):** Unknown coil — $L_1$ in series with $r_1$
  $$Z_1 = r_1 + j\omega L_1$$
- **Arm 2 (AD):** Variable resistor $R_2$ (non-inductive, in series with the arm) — $Z_2 = R_2$
- **Arm 3 (BC):** Pure resistor $R_3$ — $Z_3 = R_3$
- **Arm 4 (DC):** Standard capacitor $C_1$ in **parallel** with resistor $R_1$
  $$Z_4 = \dfrac{R_1 \cdot \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_1}}{R_1 + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_1}} = \dfrac{R_1}{1 + j\omega R_1 C_1}$$

### 3.3.2 Writing the Balance Equation

$$Z_1 \cdot Z_4 = Z_2 \cdot Z_3$$

Substituting:

$$(r_1 + j\omega L_1) \cdot \left(\dfrac{R_1}{1 + j\omega R_1 C_1}\right) = R_2 \cdot R_3$$

Multiply both sides by $(1 + j\omega R_1 C_1)$:

$$(r_1 + j\omega L_1) \cdot R_1 = R_2 \cdot R_3 \cdot (1 + j\omega R_1 C_1)$$

Expand the left side:

$$R_1 r_1 + j\omega R_1 L_1 = R_2 R_3 + j\omega R_1 R_2 R_3 C_1$$

### 3.3.3 Separating Real and Imaginary Parts

**Real part equation:**
$$R_1 r_1 = R_2 R_3$$
$$\boxed{r_1 = \dfrac{R_2 R_3}{R_1}}$$

**Imaginary part equation:**
$$\omega R_1 L_1 = \omega R_1 R_2 R_3 C_1$$

Dividing both sides by $\omega R_1$:

$$\boxed{L_1 = R_2 R_3 C_1}$$

### 3.3.4 Key Observation — Frequency Independence

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight for KTU Exams:** Note that the balance equation $L_1 = R_2 R_3 C_1$ is **independent of frequency $\omega$**. The terms $\omega R_1$ cancelled out exactly. This is the **single most important advantage** of Maxwell's bridge:
> 1. The measurement of $L_1$ is unaffected by slight variations in the source frequency.
> 2. There is **no need to know** the exact source frequency.
> 3. The bridge is suitable for measuring coils over a wide range of inductance values by using a single fixed standard capacitor.

### 3.3.5 Range of Applicability

Maxwell's bridge is best suited for **medium-Q coils** ($1 < Q < 10$). The parallel RC arm in arm 4 produces an effective **negative** phase angle, balancing the positive phase angle of the inductive arm. For $Q < 1$ (very lossy), the required $R_1$ becomes impractically small, and for $Q > 10$, $R_1$ becomes very large — in both extremes, **Hay's bridge** is preferred.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Q-factor formula** for a coil measured by Maxwell's bridge:
> $$Q = \dfrac{\omega L_1}{r_1} = \dfrac{\omega R_2 R_3 C_1 \cdot R_1}{R_2 R_3} = \omega R_1 C_1$$

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Property | De Sauty | Wien | Maxwell's LC |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Measures** | $C_1$ (lossless) | $C_1, r_1$ (lossy) | $L_1, r_1$ |
| **Standard used** | $C_2$ | $C_2, r_2$ | $C_1, R_1$ |
| **Frequency dependent?** | No | Yes (mildly) | **No (key advantage)** |
| **Q range applicable** | $Q_C = \infty$ | Any $Q_C$ | $1 < Q < 10$ |
| **Convergence at balance** | Slow (sluggish) | Moderate | **Fast and independent** |
| **KTU exam frequency** | ★★★ | ★★ | ★★★★★ |

## 4.1 De Sauty's Capacitance Comparison Bridge — Circuit Schematic

The following Mermaid diagram renders the **functional block architecture** of De Sauty's bridge, with all four arms and the detector/source identified by alphanumeric node IDs (no reserved keywords used as node names).

```mermaid
graph TD
    src1[AC SOURCE 1 kHz 5V] --> nodeA
    src1 --> nodeC
    nodeA[NODE A] --> arm1

    nodeA --> arm2
    arm2[ARM 2: Standard Capacitor C2] --> nodeD[NODE D]
    nodeB --> arm3
    arm3[ARM 3: Resistor R3] --> nodeC[NODE C]
    nodeD --> arm4
    arm4[ARM 4: Resistor R4] --> nodeC
    nodeB --> det1
    nodeD --> det1
    det1[NULL DETECTOR Headphones] --> bal1
    bal1[BALANCE: C1 EQUALS R4 times C2 divided by R3]
```

## 4.2 Maxwell's Inductance-Capacitance Bridge — Circuit Schematic

```mermaid
graph TD
    src2[AC SOURCE 1 kHz Sinusoidal] --> nA
    src2 --> nC

    a1[ARM 1 Unknown Coil r1 series L1] --> nB[NODE B]
    nA --> a2
    a2[ARM 2 Variable Resistor R2] --> nD[NODE D]
    nB --> a3
```

## 4.3 Phasor Diagram — Maxwell's Bridge at Balance

At balance, the voltage at node B and node D are **equal in magnitude and phase**. The following block diagram illustrates the phasor relationship between the four arm voltages.

```mermaid
graph LR
    vac[V_AC source voltage] --> vab
    vac --> vdc
    vab[V_AB across Arm 1] --> vsum1
    vbc[V_BC across Arm 3] --> vsum1
    vsum1[Loop 1 Phasor Sum] --> vzero1[V equals 0 at balance]
    vdc[V_DC across Arm 4] --> vsum2
    vad[V_AD across Arm 2] --> vsum2
    vsum2[Loop 2 Phasor Sum] --> vzero1
    vzero1 --> nulldet[Null Detector reads ZERO current]
```

## 4.4 Detailed Functional Architecture — Maxwell's Bridge Measurement Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    start([Start Measurement]) --> connect[Connect unknown coil to Arm 1]
    connect --> setR1[Set standard R1 and C1 in Arm 4]
    setR1 --> setR3[Set reference R3 in Arm 3]
    setR3 --> varyR2[Vary R2 until null detector reads minimum]
    varyR2 --> check{Is detector at absolute null?}
    check -->|No| varyR2
    check -->|Yes| readR2[Read R2 value]
    readR2 --> calcr1[Calculate r1 = R2 R3 over R1]
    calcr1 --> calcL1[Calculate L1 = R2 R3 C1]
    calcL1 --> output[Report L1 and r1 of unknown coil]
    output --> stop([End Measurement])
```

## 4.5 Comparative Bridge Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD

    acroot --> ind[Inductance Bridges]
    cap --> desauty[De Sauty Lossless C only]
    cap --> wien[Wien Bridge Lossy C measurement]
    ind --> maxwell[Maxwell LC Bridge Medium Q coils]
    ind --> hay[Hay Bridge High Q coils]
    ind --> anderson[Anderson Bridge Precise L measurement]
    desauty --> form1[Balance: C1 equals R4 C2 over R3]
    wien --> form2[Balance: C1 equals R4 C2 over R3, r1 equals R3 r2 over R4]
    maxwell --> form3[Balance: L1 equals R2 R3 C1, r1 equals R2 R3 over R1]
    hay --> form4[Balance: L1 equals R2 R3 C1 over 1 plus omega squared R1 squared C1 squared]
```
