If the Bourdon tube cross-section is circular with effective area 50 mm², calculate the force exerted on it at 200 °C. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Construction, Working, Advantages & Limitations (7 Marks)** **Construction:** - **Bulb:** Spherical or cylindrical, made of stainless steel, immersed in the process. - **Capillary:** Long thin tube (typically 1–1.5 mm bore) connecting bulb to indicator. - **Pressure Element:** Bourdon tube (C-type or helical) at the indicator end. - **Linkage:** Mechanical gear train converting Bourdon tip motion to pointer rotation. - **Fill Gas:** Inert nitrogen (or helium) at high initial pressure. **Working:** - The sealed system has a **constant volume V**. - As bulb temperature rises, gas pressure increases per Gay-Lussac's Law: $P \propto T$ (K). - Increased pressure straightens or curls the Bourdon tube further. - The motion is amplified and transmitted to the pointer. **Diagram:** ``` ┌── Process Pipe │ ╔════════╗ ← Bulb (N₂ gas) │ ║ Gas ║ │ ╚════╤═══╝ │ │ Capillary (long thin tube) │ │ │ ╔═╧═╗ │ ║ C ║ ← C-type Bourdon tube │ ╚═╤═╝ │ │ │ ──┴── Pointer │ Dial └──────── ``` **Advantages:** 1. **Linear scale** (P vs T is linear in K). 2. Suitable for **remote indication** (capillary up to 60 m). 3. **Wide range:** −50 °C to +600 °C. 4. No electrical power required → intrinsically safe. **Limitations:** 1. Bulb sensitive to **ambient temperature** along capillary → needs compensation. 2. **Slower response** than resistance thermometers. 3. Capillary length affects accuracy (static head compensation needed). 4. Calibration drift over time due to gas diffusion. **(Mark Allocation:** Construction = 2 m, Working = 2 m, Diagram = 1 m, Pros/Cons = 2 m **)** --- **(b) Numerical (7 Marks)** **Given:** - $T_1 = 25\,°C = 298.15\,\text{K}$ - $P_1 = 1.5\,\text{bar}$ (absolute) - $T_2 = 200\,°C = 473.15\,\text{K}$ - Effective Bourdon area $A = 50\,\text{mm}^2$ **Step 1 — Apply Gay-Lussac's Law (constant volume, ideal gas):** $$\begin{aligned} \dfrac{P_1}{T_1} &= \dfrac{P_2}{T_2} \\ P_2 &= P_1 \cdot \dfrac{T_2}{T_1} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute values:** $$\begin{aligned} P_2 &= 1.5 \times \dfrac{473.15}{298.15} \\ P_2 &= 1.5 \times 1.5870 \\ P_2 &= 2.3805\,\text{bar} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Convert to SI (Pa) for force calculation:** $$P_2 = 2.3805 \times 10^5\,\text{Pa}$$ **Step 4 — Compute force on Bourdon:** $$\begin{aligned} F &= P_2 \times A \\ A &= 50\,\text{mm}^2 = 50 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^2 \\ F &= 2.3805 \times 10^5 \times 50 \times 10^{-6} \\ F &= 2.3805 \times 50 \times 10^{-1} \\ F &= 11.9025\,\text{N} \end{aligned}$$ **Final Answers:** $$\boxed{P_2 \approx 2.38\,\text{bar absolute}, \quad F \approx 11.9\,\text{N}}$$ **(Mark Allocation:** Gas law formula = 1 m, Pressure calculation = 2 m, Unit conversion = 1 m, Force formula = 1 m, Final force = 2 m **)** --- > [!WARNING] > **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > > 1. **Temperature must be in Kelvin for gas laws.** Students frequently plug 25 °C and 200 °C directly into $P_1/T_1 = P_2/T_2$ — **this loses 1 full mark**. Always add 273.15. > 2. **Apparent vs real expansion:** In liquid-in-glass derivations, if you write $\Delta V = \beta_l V_0 \Delta T$ instead of $\Delta V = (\beta_l - \beta_g) V_0 \Delta T$, you forfeit 2 marks. The "glass" term is non-negotiable. > 3. **Bimetallic strip — don't forget the 3/4 factor.** A pure geometric derivation gives $d = L^2 \Delta\alpha \Delta T / (2t)$. The **Timoshenko equilibrium factor** is what brings it to $3(\Delta\alpha)L^2\Delta T / (4t)$. Examiners explicitly test this. > 4. **Units in capillary length:** When stem correction or class I hydrostatic compensation is asked, **forget to convert mm to m** → loses 1 mark. > 5. **Class II scale is non-linear — don't claim it is linear.** KTU has a specific 1-mark follow-up: "Why does Class II have a non-uniform scale?" Answer: vapor pressure follows $\ln P \propto -1/T$ (Clausius-Clapeyron). --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Exam** - [x] **Apparent expansion:** $\beta_{app} = \beta_l - \beta_g$ — always subtract glass contribution. - [x] **Bimetallic tip deflection:** $d = \dfrac{3(\alpha_h - \alpha_c) L^2 \Delta T}{4t}$ — note the **3/4** factor (Timoshenko). - [x] **Class III gas thermometer:** $P_2 = P_1 (T_2/T_1)$ — uses **absolute** temperatures (Kelvin). - [x] **Class II vapor:** scale is **non-linear**; follows Antoine / Clausius-Clapeyron. - [x] **Class I liquid-filled:** hydrostatic head must be **compensated** via coiled capillary or equalizing leg. - [x] **Mercury freezing point** = −38.83 °C → use alcohol/pentane below that. - [x] **Stem correction:** $T_{corr} = T_{obs} + 0.00016 \, n \,(T_{obs} - T_{stem})$ — applies to partial-immersion mercury thermometers. - [x] **Bimetallic pair:** Invar (low α) + Brass/Monel (high α) is the textbook combination. - [x] **Time constant** of bimetallic > liquid-in-glass (heat must conduct through metal layers). - [x] **ISA classification:** Class I (liquid), II (vapor), III (gas), V (mercury); **Class IV is reserved** (not filled-system). - [x] **Capillary length:** Up to 60 m practical; longer lengths → ambient compensation error grows. - [x] **Accuracy order (best → worst):** Liquid-in-glass lab > Gas-filled > Liquid-filled > Bimetallic > Vapor pressure. - [x] **KTU Hot Topics:** (i) Numerical on bimetallic deflection, (ii) Class III gas pressure computation, (iii) Apparent vs real expansion derivation, (iv) Comparison of three types, (v) Stem correction numerical.
